item.title: вҖҳThey Are Choking MeвҖҷ: Witness Describes ICE Camp Homicide That Team Trump Tried to Cover Up
item.description: ItвҖҷs been four months since Geraldo Lunas Campos was killed while in Immigration and Customs Enforcement custody at the notorious detention camp at Fort Bliss, a US military base in El Paso, Texas. After the Cuban immigrantвҖҷs death, the Trump administration quickly started covering it up , claimingвҖҰ
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
ItвҖҷs been four months since Geraldo Lunas Campos was killed while in Immigration and Customs Enforcement custody at the notorious detention camp at Fort Bliss, a US military base in El Paso, Texas.

After the Cuban immigrantвҖҷs death, the Trump administration quickly started covering it up, claiming that Lunas Campos died during a suicide attempt as staffers rushed to save his life. But in late January, the El Paso County medical examinerвҖҷs office ruled his death a homicide caused by asphyxia вҖ“ and witnesses at the camp began relaying their accounts of a handcuffed man repeatedly saying вҖңI canвҖҷt breatheвҖқ in Spanish, while being held down by multiple guards.

Today, Zeteo is publishing the account of one of those witnesses, a young man who is still being detained by the Donald Trump-era ICE. Via his attorney, he has given us permission to share his story and other details of his life. Due to safety and privacy concerns, Zeteo has agreed to refer to this detainee using a pseudonym: вҖңTexas.вҖқ

The following is based on a transcript of a conversation he had in recent months with his immigration lawyer.

In the document, Texas describes hearing the sounds of Lunas Campos apparently getting choked to death after being denied his medication; Texas also recounts a threat an officer made against him, if he or other detainees continued to talk about what happened that day. Texas is an immigrant from Guatemala who had been living and working in the Cincinnati metropolitan area. His lawyer Carolyn Marks, who is based in Ohio, has represented him since he was a minor. He has been in ICE custody for months, following a stint in a Kentucky jail after he was arrested for driving under the influence last year; there were no injuries or damage, per Marks.

вҖңThere was a homicide where the officers killed a Cuban man. It was on the news, too,вҖқ Texas tells his lawyer, per the transcript. (It is unclear whether it was ICE staff or private contractors who held down Lunas Campos.) вҖңI was there and I was trying to look through the window to see what was happening. The Cuban man was only asking for [his] medication; he said he wantedвҖҰ medication they werenвҖҷt giving him. He was insisting on the medication, and they told him if he didnвҖҷt shut up they were going to tie him up. He said, вҖҳI donвҖҷt care if you tie me up, you have to give me my medication.вҖҷвҖқ

According to Texas, there were roughly 10 detainees, including himself, in вҖңthe punishment room,вҖқ and they were listening through the wall to вҖңeverything that was happeningвҖқ during the moment of the killing. This witness recalls that Lunas Campos вҖңdidnвҖҷt calm down,вҖқ and continued asking, again and again, for the medication.

When Lunas Campos refused to stop asking for it, вҖңthe officers entered, grabbed him,вҖқ Texas says, вҖңand the last thing we could hear him say was: вҖҳThey are choking me, they are choking me.вҖҷвҖқ

This one homicide is part of a much larger pattern that has emerged out of the second Trump era: Ever since the 45th and now 47th US president returned to power, immigrant deaths in ICE custody have surged as the Trump-Vance administration ramped up its hyper-militarized, frequently lawless mass-deportation blitz across the country. According to the federal governmentвҖҷs data on detention deaths going back decades, President Trump is already breaking records on this incredibly grim front.

The death of Lunas Campos and the aftermath also underscore what is a prime feature, not a bug, of the Trump White HouseвҖҷs anti-immigration agenda: The administration has an enormous tolerance for killing and sheer brutality during the course of TrumpвҖҷs mass deportation вҖ“ and mass detention вҖ“ operations, and when things turn horrifically violent, this administration has few qualms about lying about the victims, shielding the armed agents or others from accountability, and initiating cover-ups.

Texas recalls that just a few minutes after Lunas Campos went silent, he started to hear the sound of an ambulance in the distance. Soon, he adds, вҖңit arrives at the door of the вҖҳpunishment roomвҖҷ where we were. I was in Cell 6, and a companion of mine вҖ“ who I was with in our previous cells but we were taken to the punishment cells together вҖ“ was in Cell 7. The Cuban man they killed was in Cell 8. So we were right there, next to each other. We were trying to [hear] vital signs, and the Cuban man didnвҖҷt react anymore. When the ambulance arrived, they took out the stretchers, but since the Cuban man had already lost his life and wasnвҖҷt breathing or anything, the ambulance didnвҖҷt take him; they left him there [for a while].вҖқ

Another witness previously told the Washington Post that he saw guards choking Lunas Campos, and that medical staff subsequently spent roughly an hour attempting resuscitation.

вҖңSometimes I feel scared because it easily could have been me for asking for medication,вҖқ Texas says. вҖңWhat would my family think hearing that this is happening to me? My family is sad for me, too. When I talk to my sister, she has started crying because everything happening here is unjust.вҖқ

In the days after Lunas Campos was killed, Texas and other ICE detainees at the camp wanted to find out if news of the manвҖҷs death had been reported in national or local media. He says he вҖңborrowed the remoteвҖқ for a TV they were allowed to use, and вҖңstarted searching to see if the news about the Cuban man came out.вҖқ

ThatвҖҷs when, according to Texas, one officer arrived to threaten him. In the conversation with his lawyer, Texas stresses that he had multiple вҖңcompanionsвҖқ who were with him when he was вҖңthreatened,вҖқ and could attest to it as witnesses.

вҖңWhat are you looking for?вҖқ the officer said, per the transcript.

вҖңThe news,вҖқ Texas replied.

вҖңYouвҖҷre falsely alarming people, [saying] that we killed a Cuban man, but we didnвҖҷt,вҖқ the officer said.

вҖңI heard it!вҖқ Texas insisted.

вҖңThen maybe we should move you out of hereвҖҰ Maybe you prefer I take you out of here?вҖқ the officer threatened.

вҖңWhat are you going to take me out for? I have a right to look for what I want,вҖқ Texas responded.

вҖңMaybe IвҖҷll take you out of here in handcuffs,вҖқ the officer shot back.

вҖңYou canвҖҷt do that without an order, I didnвҖҷt do anything. I will tell my family what you are doing, if you take me somewhere,вҖқ Texas said, according to the document reviewed by Zeteo.

According to the transcript, Texas was threatened during a period when the ICE personnel, as well as other staffers and contractors, were still claiming Lunas CamposвҖҷs death was a suicide. вҖңBetween them,вҖқ Texas recalls, вҖңthe officials were saying to each other that the guy killed himself.вҖқ

As Texas saw it, the officer was clearly trying to intimidate him. вҖңThatвҖҷs what he said to me: вҖҳIf I want, I can take you,вҖҷвҖқ the detainee recounts. вҖңHe told me to stop searching for what I was looking for. I told him I am free to search for what I can; IвҖҷm not tied up such that I canвҖҷt look for what happened.вҖқ

In the time since this alleged exchange, Texas was indeed moved to a different facility in Texas, according to his attorney.

It is, to put it mildly, unclear how seriously the Trump-Vance administration is taking any of these allegations, even after the cover-up fell apart in public view, following leaks to the media and the medical examinerвҖҷs report. So far, it appears no federal criminal charges were filed after the death was ruled a homicide. ItвҖҷs also unclear who, if anyone, has been held accountable. (The second Trump administration has long had plans for how to protect its killer ICE agents and federal officers.)

The Kash Patel-era FBI did not respond when asked about the state of the supposed investigation it has conducted into the Cuban immigrantвҖҷs death at the Camp East Montana ICE detention facility on Fort Bliss. And the Department of Homeland Security press office did not respond to ZeteoвҖҷs request for comment on this story.

In his transcribed conversation with his attorney, Texas claims that вҖңI have two other companions who were with me in the cell where I was before; according to them, they have been taken to testify about what they heard and everything that happened. Obviously, we couldnвҖҷt see anything because after a while they covered the windows so we wouldnвҖҷt see what happened. They covered the windows, but everything is open because itвҖҷs a punishment room; the top part is all open. So everything that was said could be heard.вҖқ

He adds that вҖңI spoke with a Mexican companion, a friend of ours, who says the FBI took him to be interrogated and to tell them what happened.вҖқ

Earlier this year, as witnesses started trying to get their stories out to the public and investigators, the Trump administration kept repeating in its official communications and in ICEвҖҷs social-media rants that Lunas Campos was a вҖңconvicted child sex predatorвҖқ and a вҖңcriminal illegal alien.вҖқ The deceased Cuban immigrantвҖҷs rap sheet is, however, irrelevant: The government is not permitted to just kill detainees in its care.

Texas, for his part, says Lunas Campos вҖңwas peaceful with all of us who were there.вҖқ

According to the young detaineeвҖҷs attorney, TexasвҖҷs US-based family, who he had helped support prior to his ICE detention, are still вҖңstruggling to get byвҖқ and that he and his family want to try вҖңeverything they canвҖқ to keep Texas on US soil, despite the odds in the face of Trump administration policy and widespread crackdowns.

вҖңHis optimism and resiliency for someone whoвҖҷs been in jail for over a year is more than I would expect,вҖқ Marks tells Zeteo.

She adds: вҖңAccording to my client, he was never interviewed by any attorneys or officials regarding these events.вҖқ

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: IsraelвҖҷs National Security Minister Celebrated His Birthday With a Noose Cake. I Wish I Could Say I Was Surprised
item.description: Birthdays are a day of joy and of love. For Palestinians, cakes are lovingly inscribed with wishes for a long life, for happiness, and for peace. For Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir, his birthday is also a day of love вҖ“ but love for death. To mark his 50th birthday, his wifeвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Birthdays are a day of joy and of love. For Palestinians, cakes are lovingly inscribed with wishes for a long life, for happiness, and for peace.

For Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir, his birthday is also a day of love вҖ“ but love for death. To mark his 50th birthday, his wife brought him a cake with his favorite symbol on it вҖ“ a noose. The inscription read, вҖңCongratulations to Minister Ben-Gvir. Sometimes dreams come true.вҖқ The cake, of course, is referring to the recent Knesset passing of the racist Death Penalty Law, which mandates the execution of some Palestinian prisoners. As his wife unveiled the cake, a popular Israeli version of вҖңhappy birthday to youвҖқ played in the background.

Why would his wife get him such a cake? Because Ben-Gvir is obsessed with killing, starving, and torturing Palestinians. IвҖҷm surprised she didnвҖҷt buy him a little toy Palestinian that he can beat daily. Oh right. She doesnвҖҷt need to; he has almost 10,000 live ones, languishing in Israeli torture chambers, that he can starve and torture daily.

Some have told me that Ben-Gvir does this for publicity; that soon there will be elections in Israel; that he is doing this to get support from his вҖңbaseвҖқ and that, as Donald Trump allegedly wrote, вҖңbad publicity is sometimes better than no publicity at all. Controversy, in short, sells.вҖқ Perhaps. But what does this say about his base?

Think for a moment about the cake decorator: the decorator undoubtedly had to figure out how big to make the noose; how to make it look вҖңrealistic,вҖқ and may have even had to look at several examples of pictures of nooses to get it вҖңjust rightвҖқ because, of course, it is being presented to a minister.

It is easy to dismiss Ben-Gvir as a right-wing fascist. It is easy to look at Netanyahu and say that he is the problem today in Israel. This has become a convenient trend among many: to focus solely on IsraelвҖҷs outspoken ministers and ignore the rest of society. But it isnвҖҷt just Ben-Gvir and his supporters who are championing the death penalty law, and it isnвҖҷt just Ben-Gvir who is so violently anti-Palestinian. ItвҖҷs much of Israeli society, as evidenced by the support for the Death Penalty Law and the recent racist videos and statements that many of IsraelвҖҷs politicians have put out.

Take, for instance, the Death Penalty Law itself, which makes the cruel punishment the default sentence for Palestinians convicted of killing Israelis вҖңwith the intent to deny the existence of the State of Israel.вҖқ (Military courts that try Palestinians have a 96% conviction rate, and thereвҖҷs documented evidence that convictions often heavily rely on вҖңconfessionsвҖқ extracted through torture and abuse.)

It is widely assumed that the law has only been discussed since October 2023; that the rise of the fascist right is a new phenomenon in Israel. But this is simply not true: Israeli leaders, including prime ministers and other ministers, have frequently voiced their support for the death penalty since 1967. In the past decade alone, the issue has been frequently discussed in newspapers and in surveys, with attempts to legislatively impose the death penalty. Since 2004, Israeli lawmakers have made 22 attempts to pass the death penalty law. For example, in 2015, a measure similar to Ben-GvirвҖҷs bill was introduced into the Knesset. It was initially supported by the government, including the justice minister. The government later withdrew its support for the bill for other reasons, and the measure did not pass, but was reintroduced several times later over the next few years. A 2017 survey found that 70% of Jewish Israelis supported the death penalty for Palestinians convicted of killing Israelis on nationalistic grounds, while 66% supported it in cases involving the killing of soldiers. Yes вҖ“ in 2017. And the numbers have only risen since.

Hearing Israelis support killing or even bragging about it is not new вҖ“ and it certainly crosses party lines in Israel. Just last month, in anticipation of IsraelвҖҷs upcoming elections (which will be held before November), two вҖңoppositionвҖқ leaders, Yair Lapid and Naftali Bennet, formed a new political party called вҖңBeYahadвҖқ (вҖңTogetherвҖқ). Ironically вҖ“ or perhaps not so вҖ“ in the aftermath of Oct. 7, 2023, everything from billboards, bumper stickers, and even stamps on eggs read вҖңTogether We Will Win.вҖқ In response, Benjamin Netanyahu and his allies put out several racist AI videos with the two leaders transforming into Arab Members of Knesset, with the message clearly telling Israelis that with this new party, the вҖңArabsвҖқ will be in charge. Of course, for their part, both Lapid and Bennet have made it clear that they want nothing to do with Arabs. God forbid!

So this isnвҖҷt just Ben-Gvir or Netanyahu. There has been no widespread public Israeli outrage over the videos or over the noose cake; there is no introspection about Israel committing genocide in Gaza or its endless, chosen wars. ItвҖҷs business as usual as Israelis once again prepare to bomb Gaza, Lebanon, and Iran, and continue to starve and bomb Gaza, ensuring its slow death, and where torture chambers are accepted (with videos of torture routinely posted as proof of being a hardworking minister). Protests are, at best, minuscule, and the purported вҖңoppositionвҖқ supports the same murderous policies. Rather, this is Israel вҖ“ where they celebrate birthdays by wishing death upon others.

Diana Buttu is a Haifa-based lawyer and analyst who was a legal adviser to the Palestinian negotiating team in the early 2000s and is a frequent commentator and writer on Palestinian and Israeli issues. She is also a practitioner in residence at Georgetown University in Qatar. She writes ZeteoвҖҷs вҖҳA Diary from a Palestinian in Israel.вҖҷ

Check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Jared KushnerвҖҷs Role as вҖҳSpecial Peace EnvoyвҖҷ Is a 5-Alarm Constitutional Fire. Here's Why
item.description: In 2024, when pressed by a reporter, Jared Kushner ruled out joining Donald TrumpвҖҷs second administration. вҖңIвҖҷve been very clear that my desire at this phase of my life is to focus on my firm,вҖқ he said , referring to Affinity Partners, the private equity company he started in 2021. Less than twoвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
In 2024, when pressed by a reporter, Jared Kushner ruled out joining Donald TrumpвҖҷs second administration.

вҖңIвҖҷve been very clear that my desire at this phase of my life is to focus on my firm,вҖқ he said, referring to Affinity Partners, the private equity company he started in 2021.

Less than two years later, he was back in his father-in-lawвҖҷs administration, and while he has helped lead some of the most high-stakes foreign policy negotiations of the last year, he might have been telling the truth about his вҖңfocusвҖқ remaining on his firm.

As Kushner claims to be representing the United States in negotiations with Iran over a war his father-in-law started with the familyвҖҷs lifelong personal friend, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, the 45-year-old is apparently planning to hit up Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates вҖ“ all foreign governments that depend on the US military for security and weaponry вҖ“ for billions in funding for his private equity firm while already managing some of their investments. Even IranвҖҷs foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, sounded the alarm, posting a screenshot on X of the New York Times headline, вҖңJared Kushner Solicits Funds for His Firm While Working as Mideast Envoy.вҖқ

The questions too few people are asking are: Who is Kushner actually working for, anyway? And is he breaking the law in his role in his father-in-lawвҖҷs administration?

Because the framers of the Constitution clearly envisioned that those who exercise significant public power would answer to the voters, Article II mandates Senate confirmation of вҖңofficersвҖқ of the United States. Although not a single person has cast a vote for Kushner, he is exercising a gross amount of federal authority as a private person. He was never confirmed by the Senate, so he cannot be constitutionally impeached. Heck, heвҖҷs not even on the government payroll. ThatвҖҷs allowed him to completely bypass the statutory and regulatory oversight laws binding federal employees, while the supine Congress looks the other way.

This is all while Kushner amasses extreme wealth by leveraging his father-in-lawвҖҷs position of unparalleled global power to his own personal ends.

ItвҖҷs a five-alarm constitutional fire. Grift aside, the fact that Kushner is playing a leading role in what is the most sensitive national security issue for the country right now вҖ“ the disastrous war that Donald J. Trump recklessly started alongside Israel in Iran вҖ“ without being bound by the ethics and transparency rules designed to protect the public interest, is an egregious affront to the Constitution, with deeply troubling implications for the nation. And people are barely even talking about it.

To understand just how corrupt and problematic KushnerвҖҷs role in government has become, we have to go back to Trump 1.0:

From 2017 until the end of TrumpвҖҷs first term, Kushner operated as a вҖңSenior Advisor to the PresidentвҖқ along with his wife, TrumpвҖҷs daughter, who was also an adviser. He was also the director of a new вҖңOffice of American Innovation,вҖқ which Kushner claimed would вҖңbring a creative and strategic approach to many critical issues and intractable problems that affect AmericansвҖҷ quality of life.вҖқ Kushner played key roles in Middle East policy, the COVID-19 response, criminal justice reform, and presidential pardons. His official claim to power? He was married to TrumpвҖҷs daughter, Ivanka. Kushner never drew a government salary. Nor did he divest or put his extensive business assets in a blind trust, leaving in place what ethicists called вҖңa web of conflicts of interest.вҖқ

Throughout TrumpвҖҷs first term, Kushner stayed in close private contact with Saudi Prince Mohammed bin Salman, a coziness that prompted three senior US officials to question whether he was вҖңsusceptible to Saudi manipulation.вҖқ According to the Washington Post, вҖң[former] Secretary of State Rex Tillerson and national security adviser H.R. McMaster expressed early concern that Kushner was freelancing US foreign policy.вҖқ McMaster was additionally вҖңconcerned there were no official records kept of what was said on the calls,вҖқ as вҖңKushner had contacts with foreign officials that he did not coordinate through the National Security Council or officially report.вҖқ

After Trump left the White House, Kushner received $2 billion from the Saudi Public Investment Fund controlled by MBS for his newly minted private equity firm, Affinity Partners, despite having no experience in private equity. That was just a drop in the bucket, however. According to an investigation by Democrats on the Senate Finance Committee and House Oversight Committee, вҖңsince 2021, Mr. Kushner has collected more than $110 million from the government of Saudi Arabia for investment management services that have reaped little to no return.вҖқ

In 2019, two White House security specialists rejected Jared KushnerвҖҷs bid for a top-secret clearance, calling his application вҖңunfavorable.вҖқHigher-ups overruled that decision. Kushner later confessed that he failed to mention four meetings with Russian officials during and after the campaign.вҖқ

Although incorporating presidential family members into the work of the White House is not new вҖ“ presidents from James Madison to Franklin Roosevelt to Dwight Eisenhower have done it вҖ“ the glaring scope of the current first familyвҖҷs apparent corruption is unquestionably without precedent. When President Bill Clinton asked Hillary Clinton to lead a healthcare task force, a scandal erupted over whether a first lady could be considered a government employee; ultimately the courts allowed her to proceed. President John F. KennedyвҖҷs nomination of his brother Robert Kennedy for attorney general led Congress to enact an anti-nepotism law in 1967.

KushnerвҖҷs outsized role during Trump 1.0 across a number of important governmental initiatives clearly ran afoul of the anti-nepotism statute, which bars public officials from appointing, employing, promoting, or advocating for their relativesвҖҷ advancement in civilian positions they serve in or control. That law expressly applies to presidents and makes clear that sons-in-law count as family members. But it has been completely useless as a bulwark against Trumpian nepotism because the penalty for non-compliance is reimbursement of the salary paid to the federal treasury, which Kushner didnвҖҷt bother to collect.

ThatвҖҷs not to mention that TrumpвҖҷs Justice Department, on Jan. 20, 2017, broke decades of precedent to release an opinion conveniently concluding that the president is exempt from the anti-nepotism ban when hiring White House employees. It cited a different law giving presidents special authority to hire White House staff вҖңwithout regard to any other provision of law regulating the employment or compensation of persons in the Government serviceвҖқ вҖ“В effectively deadening the law specifically designed to prevent a repeat of the RFK situation.

In 2022, House Democrats launched an investigation into KushnerвҖҷs glaring conflicts, but Affinity Partners refused to cooperate. The investigation went nowhere.

The first administration was bad. But the twisted legal distortions behind KushnerвҖҷs unique position in the history of American government have gotten completely out of control since Trump retook office last year.

Although KushnerвҖҷs main job title in the current White House is still вҖңson-in-law,вҖқ in February 2026, President Trump formalized the ad hoc role he had already been playing, referring to Kushner in official correspondence as вҖңSpecial Envoy for PeaceвҖқ and granting him a key role in Gaza and Ukraine talks along with another вҖңspecial envoy,вҖқ Steve Witkoff, who likewise lacks any actual legal authority to act for the US. Both were also given executive seats on TrumpвҖҷs so-called вҖңBoard of PeaceвҖқ for Gaza. Even as a special envoy, Kushner is supposed to file a financial disclosure within 30 days of his appointment. Unlike Witkoff, he hasnвҖҷt done it.

Kushner is also operating in violation of longstanding federal ethics laws. Back in 1958, Congress imposed a вҖңCode of Ethics for Government ServiceвҖқ requiring federal government officials and employees, when taking official action, to вҖңput loyalty to the highest moral principles and to country above loyalty to persons, party, or Government department.вҖқ It also mandates that employees вҖң[n]ever discriminate unfairly by the dispensing of special favors or privileges to anyone, whether for remuneration or not; and never accept for himself or his family, favors or benefits under circumstances which might be construed by reasonable persons as influencing the performance of his governmental duties.вҖқ It orders them to вҖң[m]ake no private promises of any kind binding upon the duties of office, since a Government employee has no private word which can be binding on public duty;вҖқ to вҖң[e]ngage in no business with the Government, either directly or indirectly which is inconsistent with the conscientious performance of his governmental duties;вҖқ to вҖң[n]ever use any information coming to him confidentially in the performance of governmental duties as a means for making private profit;вҖқ to вҖң[e]xpose corruption wherever discovered;вҖқ and to вҖң[u]phold these principles, ever conscious that public office is a public trust.вҖқ

Kushner has comfortably exempted himself from all of this, stating: вҖңTechnically, I have not joined the administration, so IвҖҷm still just a volunteer like other businessmen who volunteer to help the government when asked.вҖқ

Last year, appearing on вҖҳ60 MinutesвҖҷ alongside Steve Witkoff after the pair had negotiated the so-called вҖңceasefireвҖқ in Gaza (which turned out to be a ceasefire in name only), Kushner said, вҖңWhat people call conflicts of interests, Steve and I call experience and trusted relationships that we have throughout the world.вҖқ

Democratic Rep. Jamie Raskin, who is now investigating TrumpвҖҷs son-in-lawвҖҷs potential conflicts of interest, asked Kushner about the implications of his вҖңvolunteerismвҖқ in an April 16 letter:

вҖңWhen you approach negotiations related to the catastrophic Iran War, the prospect of prolonged military conflict there, the rights of women and religious minorities in the Middle East or the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, are you representing 100% the interests of your business partners in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf oil autocracies or are you representing 100% the interests of the American people? Or is it a 50%-50% split that you apportion in your loyalties between your official and corporate clients? Your client Saudi Arabia wants to see a continuation and escalation of President TrumpвҖҷs Iran war, but the American people have an interest in minimizing the loss of American lives and treasure in this conflict. To whom do your professional obligations and fiduciary duties belong?вҖқ

KushnerвҖҷs glib response? That Affinity Partners has stated that it and Kushner have
вҖқabided by all laws and regulations and will continue to do so.вҖқ

Not to worry, folks. KushnerвҖҷs private equity team вҖ“ while it solicits billions from the Saudi government вҖ“ is self-policing for compliance with the laws that apply to everyone else in government. The difference is that everyone else is banned from profiting off their federal positions. Affinity Partners is not. So itвҖҷs doing both. Kusher is no peace envoy вҖ“ heвҖҷs an embodied threat to democracy itself, with the blood and treasure of the people in his hands.

Kimberly Wehle, ZeteoвҖҷs legal contributor and author of вҖҳ Constitution in Crisis,вҖҷ is a constitutional law professor at the University of Baltimore School of Law.

item.title: BREAKING: Tennessee State Republicans Are Voting to Eliminate Only Black-Majority District
item.description: Tennessee Republicans are set to redraw and eliminate the only majority-Black congressional district in the state today. State Rep. Justin Pearson вҖ“ a member of the infamous вҖңTennessee ThreeвҖқ representatives who demonstrated in the state capitol after the 2023 Covenant School shooting вҖ“ joinedвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Tennessee Republicans are set to redraw and eliminate the only majority-Black congressional district in the state today.

State Rep. Justin Pearson вҖ“ a member of the infamous вҖңTennessee ThreeвҖқ representatives who demonstrated in the state capitol after the 2023 Covenant School shooting вҖ“ joined Zeteo on Wednesday night to explain exactly what is going on.

After the far-right Supreme Court issued a regressive ruling weakening the Voting Rights Act, Republican Governor Bill Lee quickly arranged a special legislative session to set about redistricting the state, to take the already 8-1 balance in favor of Republicans to 9-0.

Pearson himself is a congressional candidate for the Democratic district that Republicans are seeking to eliminate.

Speaking with Prem, State Rep. Pearson details the historical legacy of racist voter suppression in the South, lays out the dire stakes at hand for both Tennessee and the nation, and reflects on how Democrats failed to prevent this moment and what needs to be done now to stop it.

вҖңвҖҰif we donвҖҷt get power and use it for the benefit of liberation for everybody, we donвҖҷt deserve it. And I think in this moment, it is forcing us to ask ourselves tough questions,вҖқ Pearson says. вҖңWhat have we done with the power that we have? And what more can we do while we still got it?вҖқ

He also looks back to heroic figures of history, who help guide him in this moment.

вҖңThose folks, when they did that, they never knew my name, but they did it anyway. And what they carried in their mind and what they carried in their heart was that this country could be different and that it would require some sacrifice. It would require some suffering,вҖқ Pearson says. вҖңAnd here I am an inheritor of that legacy. And I have a responsibility to use the position that IвҖҷm in, the platform that I have, to fight for the kids whose names I will never know.вҖқ

We have removed the paywall from this timely interview. Please subscribe to Zeteo to help us do more of this work that the mainstream media fails to.

Watch the video above to hear from Pearson directly from the thick of the legislative session Republicans expeditiously threw together to dilute Black political power in the South.

Check out more from Zeteo:

Thank you for being a paid subscriber to Zeteo, your support helps keep this media movement alive. If youвҖҷd like to wear your support, donвҖҷt forget to check out

For any issues with your subscription, please email us at info@zeteonews.com.

item.title: TrumpвҖҷs America Is Not a Serious Country вҖ“ But We Are Seriously Screwed
item.description: On this day in 1994, the Norwegian painter Edvard MunchвҖҷs masterpiece, The Scream, was recovered almost three months after being stolen from the National Gallery in Oslo. An enduring symbol of existential angst вҖ“ perhaps never more relevant than now вҖ“ the painting would likely sell for hundreds ofвҖҰ
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1994, the Norwegian painter Edvard MunchвҖҷs masterpiece, The Scream, was recovered almost three months after being stolen from the National Gallery in Oslo. An enduring symbol of existential angst вҖ“ perhaps never more relevant than now вҖ“ the painting would likely sell for hundreds of millions of dollars.

Good morningвҖҰ Martin here, delighted to be writing вҖҳFirst DraftвҖҷ again. Wednesday was another utterly ridiculous day in Donald TrumpвҖҷs America, from alarming lows to social media sugar highs and back again, the WWE Smackdown presidency in full effect even while our freedoms are attacked and a major Middle East war careens from one lethal threat to another.

In todayвҖҷs вҖҳFirst Draft,вҖҷ the president talks UFC while the press tries to talk Iran, the FBI threatens journalists and Democrats вҖҰ and a private prison company cashes in on TrumpвҖҷs ICE terror. Welcome to America.

Two things can be true at the same time. Under the presidency of Donald Trump, the US has ceased to be a serious country. Under the presidency of Donald Trump, the US is seriously screwed.

On Wednesday, Trump welcomed reporters into the Oval Office. The US-Israel war with Iran remained unresolved. A cost-of-living crisis, supercharged by that war and its effect on world oil prices, surged ever onwards. With TrumpвҖҷs polls in the toilet, his party was racing to disenfranchise Black voters, as enabled by the Supreme Court.

That wasnвҖҷt all there was for the president to discuss. His administration had just unveiled a new counterterrorism strategy, while the FBI was reportedly investigating both a reporter who filed an unflattering piece about its director and a Virginia Democrat working to counter Republican electoral shenanigans.

The country was on fire. Trump wanted to talk mixed martial arts.

Surrounded by professional fighters, the president, in his baggy suit and Freudian tie, played ringmaster for his next White House desecration: a UFC event, set for the night of June 14, to celebrate AmericaвҖҷs 250th birthday and TrumpвҖҷs 80th, not necessarily in that order.

вҖңWeвҖҷre having a big fight,вҖқ Trump said. вҖңItвҖҷs never going to happen again, never happened before, and itвҖҷs all of the best fighters, best four fighters standing right behind me and all champions and itвҖҷs going to happen right in front of the White House, hereвҖҷs a picture.вҖқ

He showed a mock-up. He talked, and bragged, and when they could get a word in, the fighters talked and bragged. Eventually, Trump asked for questions, though вҖңonly for the fighters,вҖқ because the reporters would вҖңwant to ask about Iran and a couple other things.вҖқ

Among the would-be Woodwards and Bernsteins, one fearless soul spoke up.

вҖңIвҖҷll bridge this, here you go. YouвҖҷre basically вҖ“ wait, wait, this will work. YouвҖҷre facing an opponent right now in Iran that has refused to submit. You seem optimistic now that you may be closer to a deal. WhatвҖҷs different about this moment now than in other moments where a deal has seemed close?вҖқ

And we were off, into an impromptu press conference, the president discussing a major war while surrounded by professional brawlers. (On Tuesday, it was kids. It was hard to tell which group seemed more nonplussed.) UFC is not WWE вҖ“ the punches and kicks actually land вҖ“ but the вҖң kayfabe presidencyвҖқ sprang to mind once again.

ThereвҖҷs no escaping it: Trump and his administration are fundamentally unserious people who pose a fundamentally serious threat to the rule of law in the United States, to American democracy, and therefore to world peace.

Take the FBI.

On Tuesday, MS Now reported that the bureau is investigating Sarah Fitzpatrick, a reporter whose exposГ© on the bizarre reign of FBI director-cum-Trump goon Kash Patel in The Atlantic prompted amazement, fierce denials, and a $250 million defamation lawsuit from Patel, widely seen as more of a way to keep his job a bit longer than a serious legal challenge.

The FBI denied it was investigating Fitzpatrick, but the chilling nature of the report was clear. The Atlantic editor Jeffrey Goldberg warned of вҖңan outrageous, illegal, and dangerous attack on the free press and the First Amendment.вҖқ

Elsewhere, Fox first reported an FBI raid on the office of Virginia Senate President Pro Tempore L. Louise Lucas, who played a key role in passing a Democratic gerrymander to counter Republican gerrymanders elsewhere. Other outlets confirmed a long-running investigation into alleged corruption, originating under Joe Biden. But the timing of the raid seemed indicative of a central feature of TrumpвҖҷs regime: federal law enforcement wielded against political foes. Readers were reminded of the New York Times reporter who wrote about PatelвҖҷs girlfriend, only to be investigated for stalking. They were reminded of former FBI director James Comey, indicted last week for threatening the president вҖҰ with a picture of some seashells.

And then, with impeccable timing, Fitzpatrick returned to The Atlantic with a story about how Patel, whose alleged heavy drinking featured in her first report, often travels вҖңwith a supply of personalized branded bourbon,вҖқ bottles of Woodford Reserve that say вҖңKash Patel FBI DirectorвҖқ on them, which he gives as gifts.

Fitzpatrick said one вҖңformer longtime senior FBI official вҖҰ burst out laughing,вҖқ when asked if any other FBI director had given out personally branded booze. The internet burst out laughing too. But Fitzpatrick also quoted George Hill, a former FBI supervisory intelligence analyst, who said: вҖңHanding out bottles of liquor at the premier law-enforcement agency вҖ“ it makes me frightened for the country. Standards apply to everything and everyone вҖ“ especially the boss.вҖқ

вҖңWhen you degrade the office like that, you degrade the impact,вҖқ Hill noted.

Unserious, serious. Serious, unserious. And so the show rolled on.

Sebastian Gorka is an unserious man who takes himself very seriously indeed: a British-schooled booming voice (with alleged sartorial links to Hungarian fascists) who, after losing his place in the first Trump administration, took to selling fish oil and warning about krakens, but who is now back, as senior director for counterterrorism.

On Tuesday, Gorka unveiled a new counterterrorism strategy that wielded ideology over evidence, naming вҖңviolent left-wing extremistsвҖқ and вҖңextremist transgender ideologyвҖқ among primary threats to the nation.

вҖңWe see a threat, we will respond to it, and we will crush it, whether it is the cartels, the jihadists, or violent left-wing extremists like antifa and like the transgender killers,вҖқ Gorka told reporters.

Under Biden, in 2023, white supremacy was named the вҖңmost dangerous terrorist threatвҖқ to US security. Not anymore, itвҖҷs not.

For good measure, Gorka also told reporters that suggesting the US should not have attacked Iran вҖңmeans you are testicularly challenged.вҖқ

Unseriously serious. Seriously unserious. WeвҖҷre all in serious trouble.

Death tolls:

  • Iran:Iranian state media on Sundayreported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.
  • Lebanon:Israel has killed more than 2,715 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to thecountryвҖҷs Health Ministry.
  • US troops:At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.
  • Israel:At least35people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war newsвҖҰ

  • Talks talk:The US and Iran differed on how close an end to the war might be. Trumptoldreporters, вҖңWeвҖҷre in good shape, and now weвҖҷre doing well,вҖқ but also said, вҖңIf they donвҖҷt agree, the bombing starts.вҖқ Tehranrejecteda report that a memorandum to end the war was close.
  • War prep:An Israeli sourcetoldReuters the country was not aware of any imminent peace deal and was preparing for more fighting. Benjamin Netanyahu said he was due to talk to Trump and wanted Iran to lose all nuclear capabilities. Trump wasreportedto see complete nuclear surrender as a вҖңred line.вҖқ
  • Damage report:Accordingto theWashington Post, US sites have sustained more damage than reported, with 228 Iranian hits.
  • Angry allies:Multiple outletsreportedthat TrumpвҖҷs abrupt abandonment of вҖңProject Freedom,вҖқ his plan to move vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, was not strategic but forced by allies, including Saudi Arabia and Kuwait,refusingto let the US use bases and airspace.
  • Stark warning:Oil prices dropped, but a Carlyle Group economistsaidEuropean storage would run dry this month, while US tanks would empty around July 4, with three months needed to restart flows even if the Strait opened tomorrow.
  • Crisis? What crisis?Trumpsaidhe was surprised oil prices hadnвҖҷt gone higher, вҖңbut even if it went to $200 [a barrel], it would have been worth it.вҖқ Adviser Kevin Hassettsaidyes, gas prices were up, but вҖңcredit card spending is through the roof,вҖқ which he apparently saw as a good thing, not an indication of spiraling debt.
  • Beirut strike:Israelstruckthe Lebanese capital for the first time since the supposed ceasefire, since which it has continued to attack the south of the country. Israel said it killed a senior Hezbollah commander.

Private prison company GEO Group, one of the largest contractors for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), nearly doubled profits in the first quarter of 2026 after achieving record sales last year, thanks to President Donald TrumpвҖҷs lawless war on immigrants.

The companyвҖҷs latest earnings report, released Wednesday, said net income increased year-over-year by a whopping 96% in the first quarter, and total revenue reached $705.2 million.

The company said earlier this year in its 2025 report that it saw the вҖңlargest amount of new businessвҖҰ won in a single year,вҖқ with new or expanded contracts totaling $520 million. Most of that вҖ“ about $400 million вҖ“ came from contracts for five ICE detention facilities.

GEO GroupвҖҷs soaring profits come after the companyвҖҷs PAC bankrolled TrumpвҖҷs 2024 presidential campaign, which he centered on anti-immigrant rhetoric.

The companyвҖҷs corporate PAC was the first to max out contributions to Trump in 2024, according to an analysis by Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington. Since last year, two company subsidiaries have contributed more than $2.8 million to TrumpвҖҷs super PAC and outside groups backing congressional Republicans.

GEO Group did not respond to a request for comment.

In a filmed deposition in 2022, Donald Trump thought a picture of E. Jean Carroll, the writer for whose sexual abuse and defamation he was eventually found liable, was a picture of which of his three wives?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Rutgers University in New Jersey has canceled a graduation speech scheduled to be delivered by Rami Elghandour, an alum, biotech CEO, and executive producer of the Gaza documentary вҖҳThe Voice of Hind Rajab.вҖҷ

Rutgers invited Elghandour in December. But five months on, days before graduation, university administrators told him his speech had been canceled due to an unknown number of students complaining about unidentified social media posts.

In his first video interview on the issue, Elghandour took Zeteo inside his cancellation.

  • Tweet delete:TrumpвҖҷs pick for surgeon general, Nicole Saphier, deleted tweets criticizing the president and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., CNNsaid.
  • Uncivil action:The Justice Department willaskthe Supreme Court to let it intervene in TrumpвҖҷs appeal of his $83.3 million penalty in E. Jean CarrollвҖҷs defamation suit, and thereby let him off.
  • Gun suit:The DOJsuedColorado over a law banning high-capacity ammunition magazines, passed after the 2012 Aurora movie theater shooting, in which 12 people were killed.
  • No chance:The Supreme Courtdeniedan attempt to recall its judgment inLouisiana v. Callais, which gutted the Voting Rights Act and sent Republican states rushing to take away Black AmericansвҖҷ voting power. Alabamavoted to do soeven as tornado warnings sounded and the state capitol went dark. Meanwhile, Chief Justice John Robertsbleatedabout people seeing justices as вҖңpolitical actors.вҖқ
  • Epstein latest:Howard Lutnick met in private with the House Oversight Committee about his links to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Democratsquestionedthe commerce secretaryвҖҷs changing story and claimed memory lapses. TheNew York Timespublisheda purported suicide note by Epstein.
  • Debt push:The Trump administration is using debt collectors with records of abusive practices to hound immigrants over multi-million-dollar fines for failing to leave the US, The Leverscooped.
  • Serious polling:YouGov said 55% of Americansthinkthey could beat Trump in a fight, though more Republicans (39%) think they would lose to theoverweight,sleepy79-year-old than think theyвҖҷd beat him (33%). CNNsaidmore Americans believe in ghosts and telepathy than support TrumpвҖҷs White House ballroom.
  • рҹҮөрҹҮё DonвҖҷt forget Gaza:Israel killed at least three Palestinians, including a child, as they constructed a toilet in GazaвҖҷs Zeitoun neighborhood today. Another Palestinian was killed in an Israeli drone strike,Al Jazeera reported. Palestinians officials also announced that the son of the head of HamasвҖҷs political bureau died after being injured in an Israeli strike on Wednesday.
  • рҹҮәрҹҮҰрҹҮӯрҹҮә Gold returned:Ukrainian President Volodymyr ZelenskysaidHungary had given back cash and gold worth around $82 million, seized earlier this year. Such moves have followed the removal of pro-Russian Hungarian PM Viktor OrbГЎn.

  • рҹҮЁрҹҮәрҹҮөрҹҮҰ

    Americas aims:Responding to Secretary of State Marco Rubio saying the status quo in Cuba was вҖңunacceptable,вҖқ Foreign Minister Bruno RodriguezsaidUS threats to attack were вҖңinternational crimes.вҖқ Bloombergreportedthat US troops are training in Panama and eyeing other Latin American countries.

  • рҹҮЁрҹҮ»

    Cruise moves:Two infected passengers and one suspected case were evacuated from a cruise ship stricken with hantavirus before it left Cape Verde for the Canary Islands, officials said. Three people have died in the outbreak.


Donald Trump thinks thereвҖҷs a thing called вҖңspell correctвҖқ or вҖңword correctвҖқ on his phone, and it was part of the great media conspiracy against him because it changed вҖңMelaniaвҖқ to вҖңMelodyвҖқ when he posted about his wife. And he says he got вҖңthe militaryвҖқ to fix the problem. So вҖҰ that happened.

ZeteoвҖҷs Minnah Arshad and Prem Thakker contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from вҖҳFirst DraftвҖҷ by going to вҖҳ

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

And in more war newsвҖҰ

  • Talks talk:The US and Iran differed on how close an end to the war might be. Trumptoldreporters, вҖңWeвҖҷre in good shape, and now weвҖҷre doing well,вҖқ but also said, вҖңIf they donвҖҷt agree, the bombing starts.вҖқ Tehranrejecteda report that a memorandum to end the war was close.
  • War prep:An Israeli sourcetoldReuters the country was not aware of any imminent peace deal and was preparing for more fighting. Benjamin Netanyahu said he was due to talk to Trump and wanted Iran to lose all nuclear capabilities. Trump wasreportedto see complete nuclear surrender as a вҖңred line.вҖқ
  • Damage report:Accordingto theWashington Post, US sites have sustained more damage than reported, with 228 Iranian hits.
  • Angry allies:Multiple outletsreportedthat TrumpвҖҷs abrupt abandonment of вҖңProject Freedom,вҖқ his plan to move vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, was not strategic but forced by allies, including Saudi Arabia and Kuwait,refusingto let the US use bases and airspace.
  • Stark warning:Oil prices dropped, but a Carlyle Group economistsaidEuropean storage would run dry this month, while US tanks would empty around July 4, with three months needed to restart flows even if the Strait opened tomorrow.
  • Crisis? What crisis?Trumpsaidhe was surprised oil prices hadnвҖҷt gone higher, вҖңbut even if it went to $200 [a barrel], it would have been worth it.вҖқ Adviser Kevin Hassettsaidyes, gas prices were up, but вҖңcredit card spending is through the roof,вҖқ which he apparently saw as a good thing, not an indication of spiraling debt.
  • Beirut strike:Israelstruckthe Lebanese capital for the first time since the supposed ceasefire, since which it has continued to attack the south of the country. Israel said it killed a senior Hezbollah commander.

Private prison company GEO Group, one of the largest contractors for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), nearly doubled profits in the first quarter of 2026 after achieving record sales last year, thanks to President Donald TrumpвҖҷs lawless war on immigrants.

The companyвҖҷs latest earnings report, released Wednesday, said net income increased year-over-year by a whopping 96% in the first quarter, and total revenue reached $705.2 million.

The company said earlier this year in its 2025 report that it saw the вҖңlargest amount of new businessвҖҰ won in a single year,вҖқ with new or expanded contracts totaling $520 million. Most of that вҖ“ about $400 million вҖ“ came from contracts for five ICE detention facilities.

GEO GroupвҖҷs soaring profits come after the companyвҖҷs PAC bankrolled TrumpвҖҷs 2024 presidential campaign, which he centered on anti-immigrant rhetoric.

The companyвҖҷs corporate PAC was the first to max out contributions to Trump in 2024, according to an analysis by Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington. Since last year, two company subsidiaries have contributed more than $2.8 million to TrumpвҖҷs super PAC and outside groups backing congressional Republicans.

GEO Group did not respond to a request for comment.

In a filmed deposition in 2022, Donald Trump thought a picture of E. Jean Carroll, the writer for whose sexual abuse and defamation he was eventually found liable, was a picture of which of his three wives?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

Rutgers University in New Jersey has canceled a graduation speech scheduled to be delivered by Rami Elghandour, an alum, biotech CEO, and executive producer of the Gaza documentary вҖҳThe Voice of Hind Rajab.вҖҷ

Rutgers invited Elghandour in December. But five months on, days before graduation, university administrators told him his speech had been canceled due to an unknown number of students complaining about unidentified social media posts.

In his first video interview on the issue, Elghandour took Zeteo inside his cancellation.

  • Tweet delete:TrumpвҖҷs pick for surgeon general, Nicole Saphier, deleted tweets criticizing the president and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., CNNsaid.
  • Uncivil action:The Justice Department willaskthe Supreme Court to let it intervene in TrumpвҖҷs appeal of his $83.3 million penalty in E. Jean CarrollвҖҷs defamation suit, and thereby let him off.
  • Gun suit:The DOJsuedColorado over a law banning high-capacity ammunition magazines, passed after the 2012 Aurora movie theater shooting, in which 12 people were killed.
  • No chance:The Supreme Courtdeniedan attempt to recall its judgment inLouisiana v. Callais, which gutted the Voting Rights Act and sent Republican states rushing to take away Black AmericansвҖҷ voting power. Alabamavoted to do soeven as tornado warnings sounded and the state capitol went dark. Meanwhile, Chief Justice John Robertsbleatedabout people seeing justices as вҖңpolitical actors.вҖқ
  • Epstein latest:Howard Lutnick met in private with the House Oversight Committee about his links to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Democratsquestionedthe commerce secretaryвҖҷs changing story and claimed memory lapses. TheNew York Timespublisheda purported suicide note by Epstein.
  • Debt push:The Trump administration is using debt collectors with records of abusive practices to hound immigrants over multi-million-dollar fines for failing to leave the US, The Leverscooped.
  • Serious polling:YouGov said 55% of Americansthinkthey could beat Trump in a fight, though more Republicans (39%) think they would lose to theoverweight,sleepy79-year-old than think theyвҖҷd beat him (33%). CNNsaidmore Americans believe in ghosts and telepathy than support TrumpвҖҷs White House ballroom.
  • рҹҮөрҹҮё DonвҖҷt forget Gaza:Israel killed at least three Palestinians, including a child, as they constructed a toilet in GazaвҖҷs Zeitoun neighborhood today. Another Palestinian was killed in an Israeli drone strike,Al Jazeera reported. Palestinians officials also announced that the son of the head of HamasвҖҷs political bureau died after being injured in an Israeli strike on Wednesday.
  • рҹҮәрҹҮҰрҹҮӯрҹҮә Gold returned:Ukrainian President Volodymyr ZelenskysaidHungary had given back cash and gold worth around $82 million, seized earlier this year. Such moves have followed the removal of pro-Russian Hungarian PM Viktor OrbГЎn.

  • рҹҮЁрҹҮәрҹҮөрҹҮҰ

    Americas aims:Responding to Secretary of State Marco Rubio saying the status quo in Cuba was вҖңunacceptable,вҖқ Foreign Minister Bruno RodriguezsaidUS threats to attack were вҖңinternational crimes.вҖқ Bloombergreportedthat US troops are training in Panama and eyeing other Latin American countries.

  • рҹҮЁрҹҮ»

    Cruise moves:Two infected passengers and one suspected case were evacuated from a cruise ship stricken with hantavirus before it left Cape Verde for the Canary Islands, officials said. Three people have died in the outbreak.


Donald Trump thinks thereвҖҷs a thing called вҖңspell correctвҖқ or вҖңword correctвҖқ on his phone, and it was part of the great media conspiracy against him because it changed вҖңMelaniaвҖқ to вҖңMelodyвҖқ when he posted about his wife. And he says he got вҖңthe militaryвҖқ to fix the problem. So вҖҰ that happened.

ZeteoвҖҷs Minnah Arshad and Prem Thakker contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: Bari Weiss вҖҳMeddlesвҖҷ With вҖҳCBS Sunday MorningвҖҷ Story on Palestine
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this weekвҖҷs вҖҳRagebait.вҖҷ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonightвҖҷs edition, I devote the bulk of the newsletter to a вҖҳCBS News Sunday MorningвҖҷ story about the West Bank thatвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this weekвҖҷs вҖҳRagebait.вҖҷ

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonightвҖҷs edition, I devote the bulk of the newsletter to a вҖҳCBS News Sunday MorningвҖҷ story about the West Bank that sparked outrage from one of its interview subjects вҖ“ and how Bari Weiss fits in all of this. Also, Donald Trump weighs in on the passing of Ted Turner вҖ“ and gets everything wrong.

вҖңI am deeply troubled by my portrayal and the omission of key facts in the segment in which I participated in good faith,вҖқ Zaid Azhari, in a message reviewed by Zeteo, wrote to CBS News producer Sari Aviv last month.

A cultural heritage researcher and leader of the вҖңSave Sebastia Campaign,вҖқ Azhari was upset over a вҖҳCBS News Sunday MorningвҖҷ story that aired on April 5 and centered on the political turmoil surrounding Israeli archeological digs in the occupied West Bank, asserting that his interview for the piece was вҖңselectively edited to falsely portray me as someone who erases Jewish history.вҖқ

According to four sources, the report was subjected to last-minute edits and script changes by self-avowed вҖңZionist fanaticвҖқ Bari Weiss, the CBS editor-in-chief who has made sweeping changes at the network since her hiring last fall. The story had been in the works for months, and prior to Weiss joining CBS.

вҖңWe aim to inform audiences, not satisfy story subjects. ThatвҖҷs what this piece did. We encourage everyone to watch it and judge for themselves,вҖқ a CBS News spokesperson said in a statement.

вҖңOh yeah, she definitely meddled,вҖқ one CBS News senior staffer told me, while other sources noted that this represented a new front in WeissвҖҷs editorial вҖңinterferenceвҖқ at the network.

вҖңBari found out about the story and made changes to the script. She has never jumped in editorially on a вҖҳSunday MorningвҖҷ story until this one,вҖқ one CBS insider said, while a network reporter noted that вҖңthis piece is the first time sheвҖҷs pulled like a CECOT on the show.вҖқ

In their comment to Zeteo, a CBS News spokesperson did not directly address the claims about WeissвҖҷs involvement in the вҖҳSunday MorningвҖҷ story.

The CECOT story, of course, is a reference to вҖҳ60 MinutesвҖҷ correspondent Sharyn AlfonsiвҖҷs piece that Weiss abruptly pulled at the 11th hour because she felt it lacked вҖңcritical voicesвҖқ from the Trump administration, prompting Alfonsi to decry the move as a вҖңpolitical decision.вҖқ (The segment eventually aired a month later.) Alfonsi, who recently bemoaned the вҖңspread of corporate meddling and editorial fearвҖқ at the network, is expected to be let go after the venerable newsmagazineвҖҷs season ends.

Still, a вҖҳSunday MorningвҖҷ staffer suggested to Zeteo that WeissвҖҷs edits to the piece may not have substantively changed the tenor of the segment, adding that it was вҖңproblematic from the beginningвҖқ and that they werenвҖҷt sure if her involvement had much to do with how the final product turned out.

Indeed, the segment had been in the works since before Weiss officially joined CBS News as its top editor. Seth Doane, the veteran correspondent who reported the story, traveled to the West Bank and Israel in mid-September to interview Azhari and the other subjects.

Though вҖҳSunday MorningвҖҷ has largely been associated with human-interest feature stories over the decades and has generally steered clear of politically charged and controversial topics, the program decided to take on the subject of tensions between Palestine and Israel. In this instance, it focused on the escalating disputes in the West Bank over archeological sites вҖ“ which Palestinian residents have said are being вҖңweaponizedвҖқ by Israeli settlers to appropriate more land.

Throughout the 11-minute segment, Doane speaks with a number of archaeologists, residents, and government officials to understand how archaeology and historic artifacts have become so politically fraught in the area. It was his conversation with Azhari and how it was edited and framed that sparked a complaint.

Azhari told me his interview was edited in a way that portrayed him as a вҖңstupid Palestinian whoвҖҷs just a resident from Sebastia,вҖқ referring to one of the oldest continuously inhabited Palestinian towns in the West Bank and an area home to several ancient sites.

In the finished segment, most of AzhariвҖҷs remarks were contrasted with those of Amichai Eliyahu, the far-right Israeli minister of heritage who has called for the annexation of the entire West Bank and said that nuking Gaza was one option, suggesting that they represented the two extremes of the debate over the archeological digs. While the segment noted that Eliyahu is from a вҖңfar-right political faction,вҖқ the story did not mention his extremist positions or remarks.

After Eliyahu ridiculously tells Doane that the Palestinian people were вҖңinvented 60 years ago,вҖқ Doane notes in the report that it reminded him вҖңof something we heard in Sebastia,вҖқ referencing his conversation with Azhari. Discussing a plaque in the city, Doane stated that it вҖңmade no mention that Sebastia had been the capital city of the Kingdom of Israel.вҖқ

This was followed by Doane telling Azhari that вҖңon both sides, thereвҖҷs an erasure of historyвҖқ and that the plaque does вҖңnot include a Jewish history.вҖқ The response from Azhari that was aired in the piece, however, appeared to be cobbled together from different portions of the interview.

вҖңTo say this is a Jewish kingdom or a Jewish city, itвҖҷs not really that correct,вҖқ Azhari says in the segment. An additional comment, which showed him sitting in a different location, featured him stating, вҖңThis is my heritage. This is not the Zionist history or culture, this is mine.вҖқ

вҖңThis is mine. This is what we kept hearing on both sides,вҖқ Doane declared in a voiceover.

In his message to Aviv, Azhari wrote that it constituted вҖңclear editorial malpracticeвҖқ that his interview was held for seven months, only to air in вҖңa selectively edited formatвҖқ that reflected a вҖңdeliberate effort to construct a one-sided narrative.вҖқ

вҖңThis is exactly the mess they wanted from the beginning. Okay, I am from Sebastia. I am an activist, working on cultural heritage, but I am not a historical specialist,вҖқ Azhari told me in our interview.

вҖңI am not an archaeologist. I am not someone who studied history. I am just like representing the local community of Sebastia, representing the organization that is working to develop tourism and cultural heritage,вҖқ he continued. вҖңThatвҖҷs my point. I talked about it from that way. But when you are hosting six Israelis, you need to host at least one Palestinian archeologist or the Ministry of Tourism, anyone of these people who can really tell the Palestinian opinion, or the Palestinian story about it.вҖқ

He called the editing of his interviews вҖңdeceptiveвҖқ and underscored that it was вҖңcategorically untrueвҖқ that he wanted to erase Jewish history. Adding that he and the local community are вҖңfaithful custodians of all historical layersвҖ”Jewish/Iron Age, Roman, Christian, and Muslim alike,вҖқ Azhari wrote that he provided Doane вҖңwith the same historically grounded narrative we share with all visitors, yet the final edit suggests otherwise.вҖқ

Another problem Azhari raised in his email is that the piece suggested archaeology was вҖңbecomingвҖқ political. вҖңIn reality, archaeology has long been weaponized as a tool of state power,вҖқ he stated, adding that вҖңthe current settlement-driven interventionsвҖқ represented an вҖңact of segregation and erasure.вҖқ

Additionally, Azhari took issue with how others in the piece were portrayed, specifically Eliyahu and Preserving the Eternal, a settler lobbying organization described as merely вҖңan Israeli archaeology groupвҖқ in the piece. Emek Shaveh, which had two of its members interviewed for the story, was referred to as a вҖңleft-wing Israeli archeology group.вҖқ

вҖңThis is a serious failure of journalistic responsibility,вҖқ Azhari asserted in his email.

вҖңMy main mission is to have these sites safe, protected, and preserved. If it were to be done by the Palestinian Authority, fine. But unfortunately, they donвҖҷt do that,вҖқ Preserving the EternalвҖҷs Adi Shragai said to Doane when asked if the excavations amounted to land grabs in the West Bank.

In the months since Doane trekked to Sebastia to interview Azhari, and prior to the story airing, the Israeli government announced its intention to seize about 450 acres of Sebastia, having already allocated millions of dollars to develop the hilltop archeological site. According to The Guardian, the вҖңcurrent plans for development of the site involve a visitorsвҖҷ center, a car park, and a fence that will separate the ruins from the rest of the town.вҖқ This reality was not included in the CBS story.

The majority of residents in the town, meanwhile, depend on tourism at the ruins or the nearby olive orchards вҖ“ which would be inaccessible once the site is developed вҖ“ for their livelihoods. The expropriation would be IsraelвҖҷs largest ever seizure of land for an archeological project. It is also expected that a large Jewish settlement will be expanded near the site.

вҖңUnfortunately Sebastia has gone into a dark tunnel,вҖқ Sebastia Mayor Mahmud Azem said to The Guardian at the time. вҖңIt is an aggression against Palestinian landowners, against olive trees, against tourist sites and it is a violation of the history and the heritage of Palestine.вҖқ

Jasper Nathaniel, an independent Jewish journalist who has spent the past two years reporting on the вҖңarcheological warfare in the West Bank,вҖқ told me that he rejected the notion that Azhari or any other residents of Sebastia were looking to erase вҖңJewish historyвҖқ from the historic site.

ItвҖҷs вҖңsimply undeniable how muchвҖқ SebastiaвҖҷs residents вҖңtreasure all of the ruinsвҖқ and вҖңreally think of themselves as the custodians of the ruins,вҖқ he added. The occupying Israeli forces in the area, Nathaniel noted, have now made that impossible for them.

In our conversation, Azhari repeatedly pointed out that he provided all of this context to Doane throughout the interview, but none of it made it to air.

вҖңThe report just showed a Palestinian guy living in Sebastia having a very, very closed mentality, not believing there are others in this world, and the whole idea that Palestinians are not accepting of others,вҖқ he concluded.

Azhari has yet to receive a response to his complaint to CBS, which was sent over two weeks ago.

Ted Turner, the revolutionary founder of CNN and TBS who changed the face of television forever, died on Wednesday at the age of 87.

Amid the flood of tributes and obituaries lionizing the influential media mogul, lauding his philanthropy, conservationism, and вҖңaudacious visionвҖқ when it came to 24-hour cable news, Donald Trump naturally had to enter the fray. And, in typical Trump fashion, he made TurnerвҖҷs death all about himself while spewing easily verifiable bullshit.

вҖңHe founded CNN, sold it, and was personally devastated by the Deal because the new ownership took CNN, his вҖҳbaby,вҖҷ and destroyed it. It became woke, and everything that he is not all about,вҖқ Trump wrote on Truth Social.

Yes, as we all know, Turner was definitely not вҖңwoke.вҖқ That is, of course, if you ignore his marriage to Jane Fonda, his call for universal healthcare in America, his advocacy to curb climate change (which included slowing population growth), his creation of Captain Planet, describing abortion opponents as вҖңbozos,вҖқ and his founding of the United Nations Foundation.

And letвҖҷs not forget that Turner was ahead of the curve when it came to calling out Israel for its violence against Palestinians. вҖңThe Israelis ... theyвҖҷve got one of the most powerful military machines in the world,вҖқ he said in 2002. вҖңThe Palestinians have nothing. So who are the terrorists? I would make a case that both sides are involved in terrorism.вҖқ

вҖңMaybe the new buyers, wonderful people, will be able to bring it back to its former credibility and glory,вҖқ Trump concluded. вҖңRegardless, however, one of the Greats of Broadcast History, and a friend of mine. Whenever I needed him, he was there, always willing to fight for a good cause!вҖқ

Yes, Turner was such a good friend of TrumpвҖҷs, and was there whenever Trump needed him, that he publicly endorsed Hillary Clinton in the 2016 presidential election.

Watch out for my newsletter, вҖҳRagebait,вҖҷ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: 'A Lawless President': Mehdi Debates Right-Wing Pundit on Trump and the Constitution
item.description: Is Donald Trump upholding the Constitution? That was the question posed to Mehdi and the right-wing Daily WireвҖҷs Michael Knowles at a debate organized and hosted by the Dartmouth Political Union in Hanover, New Hampshire, on Tuesday evening. Held in front of a live audience of Dartmouth studentsвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Is Donald Trump upholding the Constitution? That was the question posed to Mehdi and the right-wing Daily WireвҖҷs Michael Knowles at a debate organized and hosted by the Dartmouth Political Union in Hanover, New Hampshire, on Tuesday evening.

Held in front of a live audience of Dartmouth students and academics, and livestreamed to tens of thousands more around the world, Mehdi and Knowles, a passionately pro-Trump conservative pundit and podcaster, clashed on this authoritarian presidentвҖҷs record on restricting free speech, detaining and deporting migrants, launching illegal wars, imposing tariffs, and more.

The debate got particularly heated on the issue of the Jan. 6 insurrection and MehdiвҖҷs very specific question to Knowles: did he condemn TrumpвҖҷs blanket pardoning of Jan. 6 rioters who assaulted and injured police officers at the Capitol? Mehdi may have asked the question half a dozen timesвҖҰ but he never got an answer from an evasive Knowles.

The pair also took questions from students in the audience on everything from the US PresidentвҖҷs recent clash with the Pope to whether or not Trump plans to defy the 22nd Amendment and run for president again in 2028.

You can watch their full debate above. Please do consider donating to Zeteo to support independent journalism.

Share


For more Zeteo stories:
