item.title: Trump Wants the World to Know He Cares. So Much. So, So Much
item.description: On this day in 1937, the German airship Hindenburg, the largest dirigible ever built, burst into flames over Lakehurst, New Jersey, killing 36 passengers and crew and leaving survivors terribly injured. Thanks to one of the first coast-to-coast radio broadcasts, NBC announcer Herb MorrisonвҖҷs liveвҖҰ
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1937, the German airship Hindenburg, the largest dirigible ever built, burst into flames over Lakehurst, New Jersey, killing 36 passengers and crew and leaving survivors terribly injured. Thanks to one of the first coast-to-coast radio broadcasts, NBC announcer Herb MorrisonвҖҷs live lament, вҖңOh, the humanity!вҖқ itself went down in history.

Good morning! Andrew here. WeвҖҷre told Donald TrumpвҖҷs war in Iran is вҖңa favor to the world.вҖқ His administration wants to be вҖңhelpful.вҖқ Why arenвҖҷt more people acting like it? And why does the president appear to be pulling back on his latest вҖңgiftвҖқ for all, вҖңProject Freedom?вҖқ

In todayвҖҷs вҖҳFirst Draft,вҖҷ has Trump helped too much? Plus, MAGA collects scalps in Indiana, вҖңborder czarвҖқ Tom Homan promises more mass deportations, and Abdul El-Sayed and Graham Platner find an AIPAC message they can get behind.

According to Donald Trump and his top administration officials, his disastrous and illegal war with Iran has been one big gift вҖ“ to Americans, to Iranians, and to people all over the world.

Take Project Freedom, TrumpвҖҷs plan to have the US guide stranded commercial boats through the Strait of Hormuz, a step necessary because Iran closed the narrow waterway at the start of the war, choking off the flow of oil, sending energy prices soaring, and throttling the global economy.

Trump called Project Freedom вҖңa humanitarian gesture.вҖқ And on Tuesday, Secretary of State Marco Rubio called it вҖңa favor to the world,вҖқ explaining: вҖңItвҖҷs their ships that are stranded. ItвҖҷs their fuel supplies that are strandedвҖҰ ItвҖҷs the fertilizer that they need for their food and crops thatвҖҷs stranded вҖҰ not our fertilizer, their fertilizer.вҖқ

Yes, the Strait of Hormuz was open before Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu launched their reckless war вҖ“ and sure, people are now dealing with drastically higher oil prices, and the prospect of shortages of fuel and food.

But letвҖҷs keep in mind what Rubio said Tuesday: вҖңWe want to be helpful.вҖқ

Project Freedom is just one more way in which the US has tried to help people out with this war in Iran вҖ“ thanklessly, we might add. And why is that?

When the president first announced this war, he told the people of Iran that once the US and Israel were finished bombing, it would be time to вҖңtake over your governmentвҖқ and вҖңunleash the prosperous and glorious future that is close within your reach.вҖқ

вҖңFor many years, you have asked for AmericaвҖҷs help, but you never got it,вҖқ Trump said. вҖңNo president was willing to do what I am willing to do tonight. Now you have a president who is giving you what you want.вҖқ

Sure, IranвҖҷs brutal regime remains in power вҖ“ indeed, more firmly so than before вҖ“ but Trump wasnвҖҷt only trying to help the people of Iran: He was trying to help everyone.

вҖңThey would have had a nuclear weapon and, you know, theyвҖҷre crazy, and they would have used it,вҖқ Trump said on March 7. вҖңSo, we did the world a favor.вҖқ

Yes, this favor has killed thousands of Iranians, and may have also created a scenario in which Iran becomes the sole tollbooth operator for what may be the worldвҖҷs most important waterway.

According to Trump, the US doesnвҖҷt even need the oil meant to transit the Strait of Hormuz. And yet, the US is trying to reopen the strait anyway. As Trump told Americans in his April primetime address, вҖңWe are there to help.вҖқ

OK, no, the Strait is not yet open yet, and neither TrumpвҖҷs incessant bluster, genocidal threats, nor retaliatory blockade of Iranian ports and shipping have convinced Tehran to give up its lucrative leverage over the waterway.

Should that really matter, when TrumpвҖҷs administration comes bearing gifts?

вҖңAs a direct gift from the United States to the world, we have established a powerful red, white, and blue dome over the Strait,вҖқ Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Tuesday morning.

Oddly, on Tuesday night, Trump announced on social media that Project Freedom вҖңwill be paused for a short period of time.вҖқ He said it was being shelved вҖ“ for now вҖ“ so the US can see if it can come to a вҖңComplete and Final Agreement with Representatives of Iran.вҖқ

вҖңGreat Progress has been made,вҖқ Trump added, for good measure.

An Iranian official promptly told Drop Site News that TrumpвҖҷs Truth Social post was вҖңriddled with falsehoods,вҖқ adding: вҖңWe will not participate in direct negotiations until the United States formally announces the end of the blockade.вҖқ

And yet despite it all вҖ“ despite the success, the gifts, and the favors, not to mention the brilliant statecraft of a new plan every couple days вҖ“ new research suggests that now only a third of Americans approve of TrumpвҖҷs handling of gas prices, the chief metric by which the effect of this war is measured.

DonвҖҷt they know heвҖҷs trying to help?

Death tolls:

  • Iran:Iranian state media on Sundayreported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.
  • Lebanon:Israel has killed more than 2,702 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to thecountryвҖҷs Health Ministry.
  • US troops:At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.
  • Israel:At least35people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war newsвҖҰ

  • Second stage:Secretary of State Marco Rubiosaidthe US had finished initial operations against Iran and was focused on the Strait of Hormuz вҖ“ an attempt to get around the 60-day limit for action without congressional approval.
  • Closing in?Western media, citing unnamed Pakistani and American sources,reportedthe US believes it is closing in on a one-page memorandum to end the war.
  • No joke:Hegseth and Joint Chiefs Chair General Dan Caine were asked about speculation Iran might use вҖңkamikaze dolphins.вҖқ Caine referenced an Austin Powers gag about вҖңsharks with laser beams.вҖқ Hegseth said Iran did not have such animals, but said he would not confirm if the US did. Laughter was scarce.
  • UAE hit:The United Arab Emiratessaidit had been targeted by Iranian missiles and drones for a second day running yesterday. Iran denied it.
  • Unholy exchange:After Pope Leo rejected TrumpвҖҷs latest attack over the Iran War, Rubiosaidhe expected a вҖңfrankвҖқ discussion at the Vatican on Thursday.
  • Lebanon latest:Rubiosaidan Israel-Lebanon peace deal was вҖңimminently achievable,вҖқ but Hezbollah remained a вҖңproblem.вҖқ Israel issued new forced displacement orders for a dozen villages in southern Lebanon, and far-right Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrichsaidhe had promised his son вҖңmore destructionвҖқ in Lebanon.

A majority of the Indiana state senators Donald Trump targeted for removal after they opposed his mid-cycle congressional gerrymandering push were defeated in primaries on Tuesday, by opponents endorsed by the president.

Five targeted senators were defeated, one survived, and one was locked in a tight race, NBC News reported. Another senator who opposed redistricting defeated a challenger whom Trump did not endorse.

вҖңBig night for MAGA in Indiana,вҖқ US Senator Jim Banks, a Trump ally, said on social media about TrumpвҖҷs hardcore supporters. вҖңProud to have helped elect more conservative Republicans to the Indiana State Senate.вҖқ

James Bopp, a prominent Indiana Republican attorney who was an ardent proponent of TrumpвҖҷs stolen election lie in 2020, told the Associated Press loyalty ran deep in the whole Republican Party.

вҖңRepublican voters overwhelmingly support Trump,вҖқ Bopp said, вҖңand when they find out Trump has endorsed a particular Senate candidate, they swing their support behind them.вҖқ

Trump sought to have Indiana follow Texas in redistricting to gain safe Republican seats ahead of the midterm elections, in which polling suggests the GOP will lose the US House. But the eight Republican Indiana state senators joined with Democrats to oppose and defeat the push. TrumpвҖҷs fury brought a spotlight to the normally sleepy GOP primary вҖ” and big spending, with around $12 million splashed on ads.

On Tuesday, the one Trump-targeted senator who certainly survived, Greg Goode of Terre Haute, told supporters: вҖңFolks here in Western Indiana, we made it about the district and at the same time blocked out all the noise and all of the drama while we did the work.вҖқ

As quoted by Indiana Public Media, Goode said he would address redistricting again if it returned to the agenda.

вҖңI will replicate what I did on this last vote,вҖқ Goode said. вҖңI will go directly to my constituents. I will do another town hall, and IвҖҷll continue to take soundings on it. I will say, I hope it does not come up.вҖқ

Goode also said he вҖңvoted for Donald Trump [in] 2016, 2020, and 2024. HeвҖҷs the only presidential candidate IвҖҷve ever donated to on so many issues. Most issues I find agreement.

Never one to forget a slight, however, Trump has given the senator a nickname that may stick: вҖңNo Goode.вҖқ

  • Strictly ballroom:Senate Republicanswant$1 billion in taxpayersвҖҷ money for Donald TrumpвҖҷs ballroom вҖ“ despite press secretary Karoline Leavittpromisingthat not even вҖңa dimeвҖқ for the project would come from the American people.
  • Studies blocked:FDA officials haveblockedpublication of studies supporting the safety of vaccines against COVID-19 and shingles, theNew York Timesreported. The studies cost millions of dollars in, yes, taxpayer funds.
  • вҖҳMass deportationsвҖҷ:White House border czar Tom Homantoldan audience in Phoenix: вҖңYou ainвҖҷt seen shit yetвҖҰ mass deportations are coming.вҖқ
  • JB jab:Speaking toPolitico, Illinois Governor and presumed presidential hopeful JB Pritzkersaidof Trump: вҖңI think the man has dementia. I donвҖҷt really understand how it works up there for him.вҖқ
  • Bigotry watch:At the White House, Trumpburbledto a child about transgender weightlifters. At the Education Department, Smith College in Massachusetts, a womenвҖҷs school,facesa civil rights investigation for allowing transgender students to enroll. The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission issuingtheNew York Timesfor passing over a white man.
  • AIPAC honor:After the pro-Israel lobby labeled their views вҖңa direct threat to the US-Israel relationship,вҖқ progressive Michigan Senate candidateAbdul El-Sayedresponded, вҖңI endorse this message,вҖқ while presumptive Maine Democratic Senate nominee Graham Platnersaidhe hopes to appear in вҖңmany AIPAC fundraising emails to come.вҖқ
  • Case dismissed:Reporter andself-proclaimedfree speech defender Matt Taibbi saw his defamation lawsuit against Eoin Higgins, author of, and publisher Bold Type Books,Owned: How Tech Billionaires Bought the Loudest Voices on the Leftdismissedby a judge in New York.

Pete Hegseth says Iran does not have вҖҳkamikaze dolphinsвҖҷ in the Strait of Hormuz, but the US certainly does train dolphins to detect mines and counter enemy divers. What other animal does the US military train for submarine work?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

TONIGHT at 8:15pm ET/5:15pm PT, Tennessee state representative and congressional candidate Justin Pearson will join Zeteo LIVE, as Republicans move to capitalize on the Supreme CourtвҖҷs gutting of the Voting Rights Act by redrawing and eliminating the district for which he is running вҖ” the only majority-Black district in the southern state.

Pearson, a member of the вҖңTennessee ThreeвҖң who led protests for gun control in the Tennessee state capitol after the 2023 Covenant School shooting, will join Zeteo after leading a nine-mile walk in protest of the GOP attack on Black political power.

Hear from Pearson LIVE, as he takes part in the special session Republican Governor Bill Lee arranged to expeditiously dilute Black political power in the South.

  • рҹҮөрҹҮё DonвҖҷt forget Gaza:An Israeli airstrike and tank shelling killed at least three Palestinians, including a child, authoritiessaid.

  • рҹҮәрҹҮҰрҹҮ·рҹҮә

    Strike exchange:Russian drones and missileskilledmore than 20 in Ukraine ahead of ceasefires announced this week. A Ukrainian missilehita Russian military facility a thousand miles inside the country, killing two.
  • рҹҮҝрҹҮІ Quid pro quo:Zambiaaccusedthe US of conditioning a $2 billion healthcare aid package on access to the African countryвҖҷs rich mineral deposits.

  • рҹҮ¬рҹҮ§

    Activists convicted:In the UK, four members of the banned group Palestine Action werefound guiltyof criminal damage over a 2024 raid on a factory operated by an Israeli defense firm, with one defendant also convicted of striking a police officer with a sledgehammer. Two defendants were acquitted.

This chart from Democratic data scientist David Shor is not good for the Trump administration. Not good at all. As Shor put it: вҖңJust to emphasize how bad of a hole the Trump administration is in right now, gas prices [approval rating] this morning literally plummeted so far we had to fix the Y axis of this chart.вҖқ


ItвҖҷs almost impossible to pick the WTF-est moment from Donald TrumpвҖҷs White House childrenвҖҷs fitness event on Tuesday. Teaching kids to putt, missing every one, then giving up? Teaching kids the вҖңTrump danceвҖқ? Both are contenders, but weвҖҷll settle for Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. lamenting that вҖң70% of adults are obese or overweight,вҖқ while his obese or at least overweight president fights sleep beneath him.

ZeteoвҖҷs Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from вҖҳFirst DraftвҖҷ by going to вҖҳ

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

But the eight Republican Indiana state senators joined with Democrats to oppose and defeat the push. TrumpвҖҷs fury brought a spotlight to the normally sleepy GOP primary вҖ” and big spending, with around $12 million splashed on ads.

On Tuesday, the one Trump-targeted senator who certainly survived, Greg Goode of Terre Haute, told supporters: вҖңFolks here in Western Indiana, we made it about the district and at the same time blocked out all the noise and all of the drama while we did the work.вҖқ

As quoted by Indiana Public Media, Goode said he would address redistricting again if it returned to the agenda.

вҖңI will replicate what I did on this last vote,вҖқ Goode said. вҖңI will go directly to my constituents. I will do another town hall, and IвҖҷll continue to take soundings on it. I will say, I hope it does not come up.вҖқ

Goode also said he вҖңvoted for Donald Trump [in] 2016, 2020, and 2024. HeвҖҷs the only presidential candidate IвҖҷve ever donated to on so many issues. Most issues I find agreement.

Never one to forget a slight, however, Trump has given the senator a nickname that may stick: вҖңNo Goode.вҖқ

  • Strictly ballroom:Senate Republicanswant$1 billion in taxpayersвҖҷ money for Donald TrumpвҖҷs ballroom вҖ“ despite press secretary Karoline Leavittpromisingthat not even вҖңa dimeвҖқ for the project would come from the American people.
  • Studies blocked:FDA officials haveblockedpublication of studies supporting the safety of vaccines against COVID-19 and shingles, theNew York Timesreported. The studies cost millions of dollars in, yes, taxpayer funds.
  • вҖҳMass deportationsвҖҷ:White House border czar Tom Homantoldan audience in Phoenix: вҖңYou ainвҖҷt seen shit yetвҖҰ mass deportations are coming.вҖқ
  • JB jab:Speaking toPolitico, Illinois Governor and presumed presidential hopeful JB Pritzkersaidof Trump: вҖңI think the man has dementia. I donвҖҷt really understand how it works up there for him.вҖқ
  • Bigotry watch:At the White House, Trumpburbledto a child about transgender weightlifters. At the Education Department, Smith College in Massachusetts, a womenвҖҷs school,facesa civil rights investigation for allowing transgender students to enroll. The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission issuingtheNew York Timesfor passing over a white man.
  • AIPAC honor:After the pro-Israel lobby labeled their views вҖңa direct threat to the US-Israel relationship,вҖқ progressive Michigan Senate candidateAbdul El-Sayedresponded, вҖңI endorse this message,вҖқ while presumptive Maine Democratic Senate nominee Graham Platnersaidhe hopes to appear in вҖңmany AIPAC fundraising emails to come.вҖқ
  • Case dismissed:Reporter andself-proclaimedfree speech defender Matt Taibbi saw his defamation lawsuit against Eoin Higgins, author of, and publisher Bold Type Books,Owned: How Tech Billionaires Bought the Loudest Voices on the Leftdismissedby a judge in New York.

Pete Hegseth says Iran does not have вҖҳkamikaze dolphinsвҖҷ in the Strait of Hormuz, but the US certainly does train dolphins to detect mines and counter enemy divers. What other animal does the US military train for submarine work?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

TONIGHT at 8:15pm ET/5:15pm PT, Tennessee state representative and congressional candidate Justin Pearson will join Zeteo LIVE, as Republicans move to capitalize on the Supreme CourtвҖҷs gutting of the Voting Rights Act by redrawing and eliminating the district for which he is running вҖ” the only majority-Black district in the southern state.

Pearson, a member of the вҖңTennessee ThreeвҖң who led protests for gun control in the Tennessee state capitol after the 2023 Covenant School shooting, will join Zeteo after leading a nine-mile walk in protest of the GOP attack on Black political power.

Hear from Pearson LIVE, as he takes part in the special session Republican Governor Bill Lee arranged to expeditiously dilute Black political power in the South.

  • рҹҮөрҹҮё DonвҖҷt forget Gaza:An Israeli airstrike and tank shelling killed at least three Palestinians, including a child, authoritiessaid.

  • рҹҮәрҹҮҰрҹҮ·рҹҮә

    Strike exchange:Russian drones and missileskilledmore than 20 in Ukraine ahead of ceasefires announced this week. A Ukrainian missilehita Russian military facility a thousand miles inside the country, killing two.
  • рҹҮҝрҹҮІ Quid pro quo:Zambiaaccusedthe US of conditioning a $2 billion healthcare aid package on access to the African countryвҖҷs rich mineral deposits.

  • рҹҮ¬рҹҮ§

    Activists convicted:In the UK, four members of the banned group Palestine Action werefound guiltyof criminal damage over a 2024 raid on a factory operated by an Israeli defense firm, with one defendant also convicted of striking a police officer with a sledgehammer. Two defendants were acquitted.

This chart from Democratic data scientist David Shor is not good for the Trump administration. Not good at all. As Shor put it: вҖңJust to emphasize how bad of a hole the Trump administration is in right now, gas prices [approval rating] this morning literally plummeted so far we had to fix the Y axis of this chart.вҖқ


ItвҖҷs almost impossible to pick the WTF-est moment from Donald TrumpвҖҷs White House childrenвҖҷs fitness event on Tuesday. Teaching kids to putt, missing every one, then giving up? Teaching kids the вҖңTrump danceвҖқ? Both are contenders, but weвҖҷll settle for Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. lamenting that вҖң70% of adults are obese or overweight,вҖқ while his obese or at least overweight president fights sleep beneath him.

ZeteoвҖҷs Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

item.title: EXCLUSIVE: Michigan Graduation Speaker Who Honored Pro-Palestine Students Speaks Out Amid Backlash
item.description: This weekend, University of Michigan Professor Derek Peterson delivered rousing remarks to graduates at their commencement ceremony. He honored the schoolвҖҷs trailblazers, including the first Jewish professor, who helped open the door to Jewish students; activists who pushed to expand the curriculumвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
This weekend, University of Michigan Professor Derek Peterson delivered rousing remarks to graduates at their commencement ceremony. He honored the schoolвҖҷs trailblazers, including the first Jewish professor, who helped open the door to Jewish students; activists who pushed to expand the curriculum to reflect Black history; and pro-Palestine student activists.

вҖңSing for Moritz Levy, the first Jewish professor at the University of Michigan, appointed professor of French in 1896. He was to open the doors of this great universityвҖҷs generations of Jewish students who found Ann Arbor a safe haven from the antisemitism of the East Coast universities. Sing for the students of the Black Action Movement, whose members demanded a curriculum that would reflect the experience and identity of Black people in this country,вҖқ Peterson said to a roaring crowd.

вҖңSing for the Pro-Palestinian student activists who have over these past two years opened our hearts to the injustice and inhumanity of IsraelвҖҷs war in Gaza,вҖқ he continued.

PetersonвҖҷs statement echoed remarks he made at a commencement ceremony last winter, where he celebrated those who defended humanity in southern plantations, HitlerвҖҷs Germany, apartheid South Africa, and Palestine.

Yet, his latest remarks have unleashed a political uproar. Republicans вҖ“ from those running to be university regents, to former presidential candidate Nikki Haley, to Senator Rick Scott вҖ“ have all targeted Peterson and even threatened the school.

The schoolвҖҷs own president has thrown Peterson under the bus.

What is going on? We spoke with the professor вҖ“ a historian, this yearвҖҷs chair of the universityвҖҷs faculty senate, and an elected member of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences вҖ“ to hear his side of the story.

This interview has been edited for clarity and length.

What was your reaction to the reaction of your speech?

IвҖҷm mystified at the reaction to the specific comment I made. The speech was largely about student activists. I gave it with the intention of fortifying our graduatesвҖҷ resolve. I wanted them to leave Ann Arbor with examples in their minds of people who had tackled seemingly intractable problems out of a sense of conviction, using the knowledge that they gained, the sense of mission that they gained through their university studies, to tackle problems head on. I was not speaking with the intention of being a provocateur. IвҖҷm an educator, and I teach at a public university, a university that prides itself as being the best public university in the world. So my speech was meant to give them a sense of public, political vocation, and to help them as students and graduates, recognize that the vocation of a publicly-educated student is not to go off and get rich, itвҖҷs to go off and help serve the public that, among other things, helped to finance your education.

ItвҖҷs one sentence in the context of a larger speech, but itвҖҷs that little piece that was the pivot around which a whole field of debate has now emerged, and itвҖҷs that thatвҖҷs led my universityвҖҷs president to disavow my speech. One of the members of the Board of Regents is musing about ways to deny faculty tenure. ItвҖҷs a strange predicament. IвҖҷm not a provocateur, IвҖҷm an academician. And itвҖҷs a strange position to be in.

The university president said your remarks were вҖңhurtful and insensitiveвҖқ and that they вҖңregret the painвҖқ these вҖңinappropriateвҖқ comments caused. How did you find that message?

Look, I donвҖҷt deny that people have suffered in the aftermath of Oct. 7. I have no time for apologists for Hamas. I think the events of Oct. 7, 2023 were absolutely abhorrent. The grief that it caused some members of our student body to have lost relatives and loved ones and friends on Oct. 7, I accept that is a real emotion. I probably should have found a way to honor that. I didnвҖҷt expect this to be a controversial speech.

If IвҖҷd have done it again, knowing what I know now, I would say something like, вҖҳSing for Jewish students who, since Oct. 7, 2023 have found ways to keep the memory of the innocent, dead, alive in their hearts and in our lives as an institution,вҖҷ something like that вҖ“ inexpertly worded, but I would have tried to find a way to honor the reality of the pain that Jewish students and faculty suffer in the memory of those events.

ItвҖҷs a delicate question, though, because I accept the awfulness of what happened on Oct. 7 and accept also, and am likewise horrified at what Hamas did to wound a great many people. I donвҖҷt though regard what happened on Oct. 7 as a kind of license for the awful violence that the government of Israel unleashed on an innocent people. So, to set up an equivalence rhetorically between Oct. 7 and the awful genocidal campaign in Gaza would be unfaithful to my calling as an educator. So I would have to think a bit more carefully. I would want retrospectively to find a way to acknowledge the pain that Jewish students felt, while also pushing back on [Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin] NetanyahuвҖҷs constant efforts to instrumentalize that memory in order to legitimate the campaign in Gaza.

The university president also said your remarks вҖңdeviatedвҖқ from what you shared before the ceremony. Is that true?

It is not substantially true. Like any history professor, I expound, extemporize. Having seen this statement, I went through the textвҖҰ I did give them a text in advance, about a week in advance, they looked it over. We had a dialogue between myself and administrators about the sentence that has caused all this controversy. The wording was the subject of discussion. I agreed not to use the word вҖңgenocide,вҖқ because I didnвҖҷt want to unnecessarily provoke strong emotions at occasions such as this. And I agreed not to name the specific student groups involved, but they knew that I was giving that speech. The speech, more or less, word for word, was what was submitted to them. The sentence on which all of this is hinged, I added a phrase. Instead of saying something like вҖҳSing for the students who have called our attention to the inhumanity in Gaza,вҖҷ I said something like, вҖҳSing for the pro-Palestinian students who have opened our hearts to the inhumanity and injustice of IsraelвҖҷs war in Gaza.вҖҷ So I did add, as I was going along, instead of using passive voice, I used active voice. These are things you kind of decide in the moment. I probably should have said, вҖҳNetanyahuвҖҷs campaign in Gaza,вҖҷ as opposed to IsraelвҖҷs campaign in Gaza. Retrospectively, now, a number of people have said, вҖҳOh, youвҖҷre condemning all of Israel, when, in fact, we disagree about these things ourselves.вҖҷ So I perhaps should have said вҖҳNetanyahuвҖҷ instead of вҖҳIsrael.вҖҷ And I would have probably, in retrospect, added the sentence I was talking about earlier, honoring the dead of Oct. 7.

But in answer to your question directly, the script was submitted in advance. I didnвҖҷt pull it out of my pocket and read it as if it were something that no one expected. They had seen the text, they had looked at it, and theyвҖҷd accepted that I was going to give it вҖ“ even though they werenвҖҷt particularly happy about that last sentence. But to the credit of the administration, they allowed it to go forward. So itвҖҷs not that I was tricking anybody or being dishonest. I want that to be clear. I did follow the text, and I stayed within the lines that they set, even though I did extemporize in that and in other places. There was a line also about Moritz Levy, the first Jewish professor. I added material about how he had opened the door to the generations of Jewish students who found in Ann Arbor a safe haven from antisemitism. That was something I extemporized as well. WasnвҖҷt in the text. I felt that it was necessary to add in.

What do you think it says that your remarks were this controversial?

I would like it if my university was more decidedly clear about the importance that they attach to free speechвҖҰ I donвҖҷt appreciate being thrown under the bus. The way the presidentвҖҷs statement makes it seem as if I kind of cooked up this statement out of nothing, and rolled it out with no one knowing it was going to be as it was. It was a script that I had sent in, it was on the teleprompter, more or less вҖ“ not entirely, but more or less as written. Again, I did expound and extemporizeвҖҰ What I object to is the fact that this statement has been allowed to metastasize into a whole category of humanity now thinking that I effectively tricked the university with my speech. The university has done nothing to push back against that imputation, and thus I appear to be, in the eyes of a great many people, dishonest and kind of a trickster and a provocateur. And IвҖҷm not. I was working within the structures and the processes that the university has set up. The better approach, in my view, for President [Domenico] Grasso and others involved in this would have been to affirm the value of academic freedom, and to emphasize that I didnвҖҷt speak for the institution, and if they wish to distance myself themselves from what I said, thatвҖҷs fine, but donвҖҷt make it seem as though IвҖҷm a liar. I do not like that.

Over the past two and a half years, schools across the country have been dealing with these student protests, some have cracked down on them severely. Some of these schools are also in the midst of dealmaking with the Trump administration, when it comes to speech, pro-Palestine advocacy, even practices surrounding immigration. What do you think schools are getting right or wrong about how theyвҖҷre navigating this moment?

At a place like Michigan, we rely on federal funding to keep the doors open. Our medical school commands substantial grants from the NIH. NSF is an important source of funding for our hard scientists and our engineers. We couldnвҖҷt get by without Medicare and Medicaid. ItвҖҷs a vital part of the infrastructure of higher education, especially at a public institution like this one. So I canвҖҷt lightly say, you know, [that] Michigan should go to war against the federal government and endanger our funding sources. That would be counterproductive. IвҖҷm not the one to say: burn it all down, provoke the federal authorities, force a crisis.
WeвҖҷve never as an institution been in the position where the federal government seems to regard us as an antagonist and not as an ally in the upliftment of the American people. And itвҖҷs a ludicrous position to be in. This is an institution about which all Americans, and particularly all Michiganders, ought to be very proud. WeвҖҷre by any measure, a leader in a whole range of scientific and academic pursuits that advance the collective interest of the people of Michigan and of the Midwest, and that federal authorities regard us as harboring anti-American sentiments. ItвҖҷs a ridiculous position to find ourselves in. We are an engine for the advancement of American interests. To find this great public institution at odds with federal authorities over a range of questions is very counterproductive. I would like to see a more healthy and productive connection between federal authorities and us here in Ann Arbor. IвҖҷm not here to provoke or to cause federal investigations into our university or to burn down the institution. ThatвҖҷs not my thing. IвҖҷm very much a man of Michigan. I want to retire here. I want my children to be able to study here. I want my own students to succeed. And I want federal authorities, in turn, to recognize that weвҖҷre willing to continue the work of education if they would effectively get out of our business and let us do our thing.

How has the university reached out to you since the speech?

IвҖҷve had support from the police services that have offered personal protections. You know, a number of bad actors have been in contact with me, threatening various terrible things. IвҖҷm not easily intimidated, but having police involved is a very helpful thing. So thatвҖҷs been a good thing. More generally, finding oneself in the center of an international controversy like this is not a comfortable place to be in. And as an institution, we have work to do to figure out ways to support faculty who find ourselves in a position like this вҖ“ even though, as IвҖҷve said, this particular position in which I find myself is, ironically in part, created by the president of my institution, who has effectively attributed, to my own volition, a kind of seedy grandstanding at the graduation ceremony. So some part of the intimidation that IвҖҷm now facing is the result of that dynamic. The security folks have been very helpful. More generally, thereвҖҷs been very little support offered.

Two years ago, the school had canceled student votes regarding the genocide. At the time, you said вҖңthat the cancellation of these votes sends an alarming message, in my view, about free speech at this university.вҖқ And вҖңif students engage in speech with which the university deems inappropriate or counterproductive, then the university leadership thinks itself empowered to suppress or censor that speech. This is a dangerous move.вҖқ

Do you feel as if the school has changed one way or another since that time, or does it feel the same?

WhatвҖҷs happened since then is that Donald Trump got elected. Nationally, the space for protest on behalf of folks in Gaza has become much more constrained. International students in particular, with immigration authorities looking over their shoulder, feel much less able to take on any act of civil expression about anything.
The student conduct code has been shifted around to make it much more difficult to act in a disobedient manner. This is a place where, in a former time, in the 1960s and 70s, students were rambunctious and willing to go out and occupy buildings. And administrators, to their credit, in those days were generally willing to listen to them and meet with them. What happened after the events of October 2023 and the encampment of 2024, in the wake of those events, thereвҖҷs been a steady rolling back of freedoms that student protesters had formally enjoyed, and that has been a decidedly deleterious dynamic on our campus.
For upper administrators, effectively, this is, I guess, a job well done because itвҖҷs kept us out of the news. And seen from one point of view, I suppose it is a job well done insofar as our federal funding hasnвҖҷt been the subject of any unwelcome attention. On the other hand, it has had the effect of effectively taking away from us, as a public institution, the vocation that we ought to be exercising, which is to lead around the shaping of opinion concerning Israel-Palestine, concerning racial injustice, concerning gender and inequity, concerning transgender care, all these matters around which Trump has been focusing. We havenвҖҷt been able to articulate a contrary view here in this leading public university, except through statements and other sort of polite forms of protest, and thatвҖҷs a real problem.

Do you see this backlash and controversy surrounding yourself as a continuance of that suppression you had spoken of two years ago?

I donвҖҷt want to make myself a martyr, Prem. IвҖҷm not anybodyвҖҷs martyr. IвҖҷm a tenured professor. IвҖҷm not going to lose my job. If they went after my tenureвҖҰ Michigan is not going to scupper tenure over this. So thereвҖҷs much less at stake for me than there are for student protesters, for international students, for contingent faculty who have had to bear the brunt, over the course of years, of the universityвҖҷs efforts to really clamp down on free speech. So I donвҖҷt want to put myself in that heroic tradition, that admirable tradition, per se. I think that whatвҖҷs happening to me is of a piece with what happened, what has been happening to student protesters, but the consequences for them have been much more severe. I have no expectation that anything like what has happened to student protesters will happen to me. If it does, then we will have another conversation.

ItвҖҷs been two and a half years of the genocide. Two years ago, you were speaking on the possible risks of a university suppressing its studentsвҖҷ free speech. Here we are two years later, in which tens of thousands more people have been killed, and yet weвҖҷre still having gradations of this same conversation about academic freedom. I wonder if you have any reflections on that.

I study colonized societies in Africa. My first book was about the history of central Kenya, where, in the 1950s, the British interned a whole category of humanity, the Kikuyu-speaking people, as part of their effort to combat an insurgency called Mau Mau. Hundreds of thousands of Kikuyu people were interned and tortured, and thereafter, the British set about covering up what they had done in Kenya by destroying files, by hiding things away in a military installation in England. I used to teach at Cambridge University. About 15 years ago, colleagues there were able to get the British to release records that illuminated what had happened in the 1950s during the anti-Mau Mau war. So for several generations, people who had suffered, there was no public recognition of what had happened to them, either from the British or from their own Kenyan government, which, likewise, had participated in this cover-up. So the trauma of what happened in the 1950s effectively was written out of history.

Likewise, IвҖҷve just published a book about the Idi Amin government of the 1970s, where circumspect and careful journalists made a point of eliding evidence of violence so as to save their own hides, and to effectively cover up, not through acts of volition, but out of a sense of self-interest, what was happening in AminвҖҷs Uganda. So as IвҖҷve been faculty senate chair this year, IвҖҷve made a point of not participating through acts of commission in the invisibilizing of Palestinian suffering. That is, IвҖҷve made a point of trying over this past year or two, since IвҖҷve been in faculty government, in my communications with faculty, in my conversations with university authorities, and our dialogues with students, to try to acknowledge Michigan is a place where thereвҖҷs a very substantial proportion of students who come from Dearborn, AmericaвҖҷs leading Arab American city, who have got relatives in Gaza, and who have lost folks there over the course of this awful genocidal campaign.

I think the important work that folks with the protections of academic freedom can do is to refuse to bend the knee to the willful forgetting, the willful covering up of what happened in Gaza. And itвҖҷs not to say that itвҖҷs unknown, but it has become an unsayable subject, as this particular controversy illuminates. The cost of talking about Gaza is so obviously high, for me, one of the most eminent faculty members at the University of Michigan вҖ“ IвҖҷm not trying to toot my own horn, but the university has an interest in ensuring that my reputation as a scholar is not undermined вҖ“ and yet theyвҖҷve found it within themselves to effectively throw me under the bus. And that does show you that there are reasons why IвҖҷm worried about how this dynamic is effectively taking American academics and European academics entirely out of the work of documenting, publicizing, and raising consciousness of what happened in Gaza.

Have any tips or information for us? You can text Prem on Signal at premthakker.35.

item.title: I Was Beaten and Kidnapped by Israeli Soldiers When They Illegally Intercepted the Gaza Flotilla
item.description: вҖңAlex, now, itвҖҷs happening now. TheyвҖҷre approaching. Get up here.вҖқ That was the captain of the boat I was sailing on with the Global Sumud Flotilla (GSF) as Israeli forces approached last Wednesday evening. We had anticipated Israel would try to attack or intimidate the boats to deter the flotillaвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
вҖңAlex, now, itвҖҷs happening now. TheyвҖҷre approaching. Get up here.вҖқ

That was the captain of the boat I was sailing on with the Global Sumud Flotilla (GSF) as Israeli forces approached last Wednesday evening.

We had anticipated Israel would try to attack or intimidate the boats to deter the flotilla from continuing its journey. It had deployed drones against GSF boats around the same area during the last mission. But more than 600 miles away from Gaza and in international waters, we did not expect that Israel would board the boats and abduct crew members so far outside of their self-designated вҖңsecurityвҖқ perimeter 172 miles from GazaвҖҷs shores. In any event, thereвҖҷs no justifiable security concern that should stop civilian-led efforts to help support reconstruction and to provide a protective presence in Gaza, where massacres, famine, and catastrophic health conditions continue to kill Palestinians every day and where no foreign and independent journalists are permitted to enter and document conditions on the ground.

By the end of the night, Israel had intercepted 22 boats and kidnapped around 180 activists, humanitarians, and journalists, like myself on board, while beating a number of us вҖ“ with limited global reaction to IsraelвҖҷs aggression against the flotilla, the ongoing imprisonment of Saif Abukeshek and Thiago ГҒvila by Israeli authorities, and the daily horrific violence inflicted on Palestinians in Gaza and the West Bank.

The surprise Israeli interception happened within minutes.

For 2 hours, after the sun had set, our ship had made evasive maneuvers away from Israeli forces, which included a large military frigate. Then things went quiet as we headed to the new waypoint. I had returned to the cabin of our sailboat to gather the latest internal reports of Israeli naval vessels that had been harassing the flotilla. Before I knew it, the captain was yelling for me to get to the cockpit, and Israeli soldiers were threatening to shoot us if we didnвҖҷt turn off the engine and stop the ship.

I could see the gunsвҖҷ green lights on the chest of the crew member sitting opposite me in the cockpit. I went to livestream the interception, but the internet had been cut. The Israeli soldiers boarded and immediately broke the CCTV camera onboard and then demanded that we show them any other cameras and the shipвҖҷs Starlink for the internet. They put a gun to the captainвҖҷs head and told him вҖңto drive toward the light,вҖқ where the Israeli military ship was on the horizon.

An Israeli soldier demanded that I stand up, and I was pulled out of the cockpit. I was then searched, and the Israeli soldier found my phone, saying, вҖңNice, a phone, bad for you!вҖқ as he put it in his cargo pants pocket. He then zip-tied my hands and brought me to the bow of the boat, crooking my arms behind my back, forcing me to lie prone on my knees with his hands on my neck. He did the same with the rest of the crew and the captain, after the Israeli forces commandeered the vessel. I was brought inside to вҖңpack a bag,вҖқ and when I attempted to hide my camera and tablet, the Israeli soldier slammed something against my back and stole the devices and the bag with my cash and debit cards.

We were then gathered into one room in the cabin, and some of us smoked a final cigarette, but separate smoke had started coming in from under the door from outside: the Israelis had gunned our faulty engine, which we had already fixed twice during the voyage, and the whole thing had gone up in smoke. I ran to the cockpit and demanded that they let us out of the cabin. Engine smoke billowing out of the cabin was the last I saw of the ship I had been traveling on since April 15, as the Israelis took us hostage on their Zodiac vessel. We were then loaded onto an American-made Israeli frigate called Nahshon, where we found the other participants who had just gone through something similar.

Once on the Israeli frigate, we were forced to lie prone and on our knees on the cold and wet ship floor in clumps of people, with Israeli soldiers placing their boots on our heads and necks. We were then forced to hand over our bags and belongings вҖ“ things that were never returned вҖ“ and we were taken through processing. When taken through a container emblazoned with an Israeli flag, I was patted down and then pushed against the container wall, as one soldier that I couldnвҖҷt see hit me in my back and ribs. I was then led into the makeshift prison yard, surrounded by three shipping containers to hold those who had been detained, and a separate container that served as an isolating confinement cell вҖ“ where many were sometimes led, and where participants reported they were regularly abused while we were held by Israeli forces for over 40 hours.

At first, participants were relieved to find everyone had been gathered in the same place, and people tried to keep morale high in the early hours of our detainment. That mood waned as the Israeli threats increased and the conditions became more severe between the heat exhaustion, floodlights, soaked sponge mattresses, and little to no food or water. Israeli soldiers picked off people one at a time and took them away to beat them. Each time they took someone, everyone else protested, refusing to cooperate with the soldiers until the other participants were returned. These collective demands were sometimes met, but not when GSF leader Thiago ГҒvila and Saif Abukeshek were taken. By the morning of the second day, we had been told we were leaving the ship. We didnвҖҷt know to where: we had assumed we were sailing east to Ashdod in Israel, before realizing we were actually headed to Greece. When participants told Israeli soldiers they wouldnвҖҷt leave without Saif and Thiago, soldiers pulled people out by force and brutally beat those who refused to cooperate. We would later learn that the two GSF leaders were taken to Israel and are facing bogus accusations of being affiliated with a terrorist group.

Eventually, the Greek Coast Guard brought us to the port of Atherinolakkos on Crete. Several people needed medical attention: one person had an injured leg and couldnвҖҷt walk, several people had concussion symptoms, several more had large facial contusions, at least four people had bruised or fractured ribs, while at least one person required an ambulance, according to Marie, a registered nurse from Canada and medic onboard. But the medical attention was delayed, as Greek authorities held everyoneвҖҷs passports. Eventually, we were released and offered shelter, clothes, and food by local residents. Roberto, one participant who had been in the hospital since docking in Greece, was just released on Tuesday, after he had been treated for six broken ribs.

Around 30 GSF ships that werenвҖҷt intercepted, including Open Arms and Greenpeace, remain anchored in the port of Ierapetra. Many of the participants who had been detained have since returned to their respective countries, but enough remain to sail the 30 ships for the next leg of the voyage. A general assembly will be held on Tuesday and Wednesday to make final decisions about what happens next, and itвҖҷs unclear if the tentative May 7 departure date and potential sail toward Turkey will hold.

On Monday night, boats sailing with the Freedom Flotilla Coalition on a similar mission that had set sail from Sicily over the weekend reported seeing a nearby ship, a helicopter, and drones overhead. There are fears that Israel is preparing to intercept those boats and abduct those on board, as it did the GSF boats just days earlier.

Israel and its supporters, namely the United States, have tried to paint participants on both missions as terrorist-supporting criminals, but these ships are carrying nothing more than people and humanitarian supplies. Participants work hard to coordinate dozens of faulty boats and hundreds of committed sailors with the simple mission of putting more attention on the genocide in Gaza, while aiming to break the Israeli siege and blockade to bring much-needed supplies to the enclave and to assist with the Palestinian-led reconstruction efforts.

It was, after all, Israel that boarded the ships вҖ“ an unlawful act of piracy in international waters, hundreds of miles away from its territory вҖ“ and took unarmed and nonviolent people hostage by force and under threat of death.

Zeteo contributor Alex Colston is an independent journalist, editor, educator, and political organizer. He lives in Brooklyn, New York, but he prefers to be at sea.

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: Trump's 'Mini-War' in Iran Is... Back
item.description: On this day in 1821, Napoleon Bonaparte died in exile on Saint Helena. The former emperor of France was sent to the bleak island in the southern Atlantic Ocean in October 1815, having refused to accept the end of his time in power. Just saying. Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you aвҖҰ
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1821, Napoleon Bonaparte died in exile on Saint Helena. The former emperor of France was sent to the bleak island in the southern Atlantic Ocean in October 1815, having refused to accept the end of his time in power. Just saying.

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Tuesday, wherever you are. And I am sending warm wishes to you all in Iran and the Gulf, because the so-called вҖңceasefire,вҖқ as with the other such вҖңceasefiresвҖқ over the past two-and-a-half years, is proving to be fleetingвҖҰ

In todayвҖҷs вҖҳFirst Draft,вҖҷ Donald Trump canвҖҷt decide if hostilities are вҖңterminatedвҖқ вҖ“ let alone if those hostilities were вҖңwar,вҖқ a вҖңmini-war,вҖқ or a вҖңspecial operation.вҖқ Also: new polling from the New York suburbs shows how far left rank-and-file Democrats have moved on Israel and Palestine вҖ“ and yet, the party machine is intervening in California to boost a candidate who said Israel was committing genocide in Gaza and thenвҖҰ changed her mind.

Days after President Donald Trump claimed hostilities against Iran had been вҖң terminated,вҖқ that seems, predictably, anything but the case, as the US, Iran, and the United Arab Emirates attack and counterattack over the Strait of Hormuz.

Trump made last weekвҖҷs claim that fighting had stopped in order to avoid having to seek congressional authorization for his war, as he passed a 60-day deadline to do so, per the War Powers Resolution of 1973.

Republicans in control of Congress show no sign of holding Trump to that limit, but nonetheless, his logic was spurious, as he claimed that time during a ceasefire didnвҖҷt count towards the 60-day limit. Moreover, Trump claimed that the war was вҖңterminatedвҖқ in the middle of a massive deployment of troops and resources, in part to help maintain a blockade of Iranian ports and shipping in response to its closure of the Strait of Hormuz вҖ“ a move that only took effect after Trump attacked Iran, and which has tanked global markets, causing oil prices to surge and restricting the flow of agricultural products.

On Sunday, Trump announced вҖңProject Freedom,вҖқ his plan to have US vessels escort stranded ships out of the Strait. He called the operation a вҖңHumanitarian gesture on behalf of the United States, Middle Eastern Countries but, in particular, the Country of Iran,вҖқ and warned that any interference would вҖңunfortunately, have to be dealt with forcefully.вҖқ

IranвҖҷs national security and foreign policy chief, Ebrahim Azizi, threatened retaliation.

вҖңAny American interference in the new maritime regime of the Strait of Hormuz will be considered a violation of the ceasefire,вҖқ Azizi said. вҖң

The Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf would not be managed by TrumpвҖҷs delusional posts!вҖқ he added. вҖңNo one would believe Blame Game scenarios!вҖқ

On Monday, inevitably it seemed, the US and Iran exchanged fire. Iranian vessels attacked US ships but did not hit them, according to Admiral Bradley Cooper of US Central Command, who said the US вҖңblew upвҖқ six Iranian boats.

If fire ever ceased, it is no longer so.

Iran said the US did not sink any of its boats. It also claimed it struck a US ship, which the US denied. Elsewhere, Iran attacked an oil industry zone in the UAE, reportedly injuring three Indian workers. It was the first attack in the Emirates in nearly a month.

вҖңEvents in Hormuz make clear that thereвҖҷs no military solution to a political crisis,вҖқ Iranian Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Araghchi said.

вҖңAs talks are making progress with PakistanвҖҷs gracious effort, the US should be wary of being dragged back into quagmire by ill-wishers. So should the UAE,вҖқ he added.

вҖңProject Freedom is Project Deadlock.вҖқ

With tensions rising, Trump reverted to his safe space: threatening war crimes , in this case by blowing Iran вҖң

In White House remarks meant to honor Small Business Week, Trump got his words in a twist.

First, he called the US-Israel war on Iran a вҖңmilitary operation,вҖқ a вҖңlittle detourвҖқ he claimed was вҖңworking out very nicely.вҖқ

Seconds later, he called it a вҖңwar.вҖқ

A few minutes later, he complained: вҖңWe have a war right now and weвҖҷre to what, six weeks?вҖң(Actually, itвҖҷs more like nine.) вҖңThey said, вҖҳWhatвҖҷs taking so long!вҖҷ We were in Vietnam 19 years. We were in Iraq for many years, 10 years, 12 years.вҖқ

Trump concluded: вҖңWeвҖҷre in вҖ“ I call it a mini-war.вҖқ

So much for вҖңterminatedвҖқ hostilities. And in case you were wondering about that 60-day deadline for congressional approval of military actionвҖҰ Congress is in recess. Again.

Death tolls:

  • Iran:Iranian state mediareported, citing officials, that US-Israeli attacks have killed 3,468 people.
  • Lebanon:Israel has killed more than 2,696 people, including at least 177 children, in Lebanon, according to thecountryвҖҷs Health Ministry.
  • US troops:At least 13 US troops have died in incidents related to the war.
  • Israel:At least35people, including 12 Israeli soldiers, have been killed, according to officials.

And in more war newsвҖҰ

  • Epic fail:Analystssaidthe relapse into violence around the Strait of Hormuz showed that far from being вҖңone of the greatest military maneuvers ever done,вҖқ as Trumpclaimedto Fox, the US blockade of Iranian ships and ports had failed.
  • Beat the bomb:US intelligence estimates of how long it would take Iran to build a nuclear weapon have not been changed by the war, including Israeli strikes on nuclear facilities, Reutersreported. The timeline is still one year, sources said.
  • Oil up:News that an Iranian missile hit the largest oil storage facility in the UAE prompted anothersurgein prices, with Brent crude going above $119 a barrel.
  • Plane simple:In the US, the defunct Spirit Airlinessaidthe rising price of aviation fuel amid the Iran war sealed its collapselast week.
  • Lebanon strikes:The Lebanese army said a police officer and a soldier were injured in an Israeliattackon Kfarkela in the south of the country, as Israel continues to violate a nominal ceasefire.

New polling conducted by Data for Progress and commissioned by the campaign of Democratic candidate Effie Phillips-Staley shows astonishing numbers in New YorkвҖҷs 17th US House district. ItвҖҷs one of a handful of seats that voted for Democratic nominee Kamala Harris and a House Republican (Mike Lawler) in 2024, but the numbers underscore seismic changes among rank-and-file Democratic voters.

In the suburban district outside New York City, according to the new poll, Democrats sympathize more with Palestinians than with Israelis by a 26-point margin.

The pro-Palestine New York mayor, Zohran Mamdani, enjoys +64 favorability, while the pro-Israel lobby AIPAC has a -32 unfavorability. In other words, in the US House district that is home to Bill and Hillary Clinton, Mamdani has a +96 favorability over a lobby group that supported the Democratic former first couple.

Lawler, the sitting Republican congressman, recently introduced a resolution to condemn the progressive streamer Hasan Piker. In the Data for Progress poll, most Democrats in LawlerвҖҷs district didnвҖҷt know enough about Piker to give an opinion, but among Democrats who did, he had a +1 favorability rating.

In the Democratic primary, most voters say they have not yet heard of Phillips-Staley, who weathered attacks from some party officials after visiting the West Bank, where Israeli settlers harassed her, a priest, and Jewish activists.

But according to the poll, after voters receive a full briefing on Phillips-Staley, she leads the primary to take on Lawler, surpassing opponents, including one calling for the US to give Israel even more money every year, and another who has worked with an AI firm that says it supports the Trump Department of Homeland Security and tech surveillance giant Palantir.

Read more on the pollвҖҷs fascinating findings here.

  • DC shooting:After law enforcement shot a person seen with a gun near the White House, the executive mansion was brieflylocked down. Regardless, Donald Trump gave abizarre, rambling speechat a Small Business Week event.
  • No joke:Horrendous pollingbe damned вҖ“ Trump is still talking about staying past the end of his second term. вҖңWhen I get out of office in letвҖҷs say eight or nine years from now,вҖқ the presidentsaid, to laughter and applause.
  • No probes:The Department of Homeland Security is closing its office for investigating immigration system misconduct and abuse, HuffPoreported.
  • Pill ruling:The Supreme Courttemporarilyrestored access to the abortion pill mifepristone, while arguments continue over a lower-court order blocking it.
  • Fix is in:The far-right Court alsofast-trackedits judgment inLouisiana v. Callais, clearing the way for state Republicans to rip up their congressional map and torch Black representation, even though primary voting has already started.
  • Bains boost:The Democratic Congressional Campaign CommitteeendorsedHouse candidates, including Jasmeet Bains of California, whosaidIsrael was committing genocide in Gaza, before changing her mind and receiving pro-Israel support.
  • Prize parade:The Pulitzer Prize for Public Service wasawarded, for coverage of the Trump administrationвҖҷs brutal cuts to the federal workforce, to theWashington Post, which recently brutally cut its workforce.

Before the ratification of the 22nd Amendment in 1951, limiting US presidents to two elected terms, two presidents actively sought a third term in office but failed to secure their partyвҖҷs nomination. Who?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • рҹҮөрҹҮё DonвҖҷt forget Gaza:At least two people were killed in Israeli strikes, the Wafa news agencyreported, including one in Bureij refugee camp and one southeast of Gaza City. Israel has killed more than 800 people since the so-called ceasefire agreement was signed last October.

  • рҹҮәрҹҮҰрҹҮ·рҹҮә

    Competing ceasefires:Russiasaidit would temporarily cease hostilities against Ukraine on May 8-9, as it marks Victory Day, its traditional celebration of winning World War Two. Ukraine said it would cease attacks on Russia on May 6-7.

  • рҹҮЁрҹҮ»

    Cruise control:A cruise ship stricken by the deadly hantavirus and with 150 people aboard was anchored off Cape Verde,awaiting help. Three people are confirmed to have died in the outbreak of the rodent-borne disease.
  • рҹҮёрҹҮ© Drone downed:The Sudanese militaryclaimedit foiled an attack on the main Khartoum airport by paramilitary forces, amid the four-year civil war. On Saturday, at least five people were killed by a drone attack on the outskirts of the city.


On one level, this is the usual bizarre word salad, a rapidly aging president reversing his own meaning by boasting that вҖңwith one swipe of the pen, I could say, let's have no employment.вҖқ On another level, Donald Trump proceeds to callously brag about вҖңterminat[ing] a lot of federal jobs.вҖқ HowвҖҷre those Virginia US House seats looking, Don?

ZeteoвҖҷs Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from вҖҳFirst DraftвҖҷ by going to вҖҳ

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

The former emperor of France was sent to the bleak island in the southern Atlantic Ocean in October 1815, having refused to accept the end of his time in power. Just saying. Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you aвҖҰ

item.title: How to Beat the Far Right: Lessons From History
item.description: The ideas of the far right, once confined to the margins of politics, have gone mainstream. A decade ago, it was just about possible to think that the right-wing nationalism sweeping through liberal democracies around the world might subside of its own accord. Whatever discontents it profited fromвҖҰ
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The ideas of the far right, once confined to the margins of politics, have gone mainstream. A decade ago, it was just about possible to think that the right-wing nationalism sweeping through liberal democracies around the world might subside of its own accord. Whatever discontents it profited from could, perhaps, be dealt with or absorbed back into mainstream politics. Or perhaps its figureheads would show themselves to be so incompetent at governing that voters wouldnвҖҷt give them a second chance. That is clearly not the case.

As the political analyst Mark Leonard argues in a recent study, this is a movement that has attuned itself to the times we live in. In the West, each new shock, from the rising costs of energy and food to increasing global conflict, falls on the shoulders of communities whose resilience has been hollowed out by an economic system in which inequality has been allowed to soar and the public good disdained. Incumbent elites are forced into defending the existing, crumbling order, even though many of them privately admit it isnвҖҷt working.

Yet nowhere have right-wing nationalists gained the support of an overwhelming majority вҖ“ or in many cases, even a majority at all. So where does that leave us?

The good news is that people all over the world have been grappling with this question for some years now. Nobody has a complete answer, but there are several lessons to draw from whatвҖҷs been tried, what worked, and what didnвҖҷt:

Years of writing about this subject have taught me that there is no carefully worked-out master plan behind the rise of far-right politics. Its movements are riven with contradictions вҖ“ for instance, between the economic aspirations of many of their supporters and the financial interests of party backers. Populist demagogues are adept at colonizing our attention in an algorithmically distorted media landscape, but they are often just making it up as they go along. WhatвҖҷs more, in the way they define the вҖҳpeopleвҖҷ and the вҖҳelites,вҖҷ it is clear there are millions of us who fall outside either category.

In each of these cases, the far rightвҖҷs strong points can be turned back against it. The story I always turn to when IвҖҷm thinking about this comes from Poland. In October 2020, the constitutional court вҖ“ which the far-right Law and Justice government had stuffed with friendly judges вҖ“ tightened already strict abortion laws. In effect, it made abortion illegal even when the fetus had a severe and permanent disability or an incurable and life-threatening disease.

For years, Law and JusticeвҖҷs opponents had criticized its purging of independent minds in PolandвҖҷs judiciary, but the government had always been able to brush this off as an elite concern. Law and Justice thought its Catholic conservatism was what вҖҳthe peopleвҖҷ wanted. Only in this case, it turned out they didnвҖҷt. Hundreds of thousands of protesters, led by the women whose bodily freedoms were affected by the new law, came out into the streets to protest against the government. The protests, which united under the name вҖҳwomenвҖҷs strike,вҖҷ demanded abortion rights, the restoration of independent courts, and an end to the ChurchвҖҷs influence on politics and education.

The government tried to ignore the protests, then it tried to suppress them. The organizers were threatened with prosecution, while far-right militias supportive of the government attacked protesters and dragged women who held sit-ins in churches out onto the street. But the protests kept going, and it became harder to deny they were a mass movement, rooted in peopleвҖҷs everyday concerns as well as moral principle: a womanвҖҷs right to choose is both.

This message was carried further by protest slogans that expressed both anger at this attempt to restrict womenвҖҷs rights, and mockery of the politicians who claimed to speak for Polish women: вҖңMy body is not a coffinвҖқ; вҖңThe government is not a pregnancy, it can be removedвҖқ; вҖңThe cat can stayвҖқ вҖ“ a reference to the Law and Justice leaderвҖҷs pet cat вҖ“ вҖңthe government get the fuck out.вҖқ The energy mobilized by these protests fed into a record high turnout, particularly among young people, at the general election of 2023 that saw Law and Justice kicked out of office.

This isnвҖҷt a template to copy: rather, itвҖҷs a striking example of where people linked a whole set of abstract issues to one of immediate, material concern. An issue, in fact, that goes right to the heart of right-wing nationalism itself: womenвҖҷs bodies, which it treats as vessels for national renewal. What other issues are out there that allow us to call the far rightвҖҷs bluff, and break its monopoly of attention?

The far right fills a vacuum: an absence of challenge to racist and divisive arguments; the holes in the social fabric left by years of failed economic policies; a sense that thereвҖҷs no better option. It is adept at summoning the emotions and making its arguments sound like common sense. Its opponents need to do that too.

A few years ago, for his book If We Burn, the journalist Vincent Bevins traveled the world to talk to people who had taken part in the mass protest movements of the 2010s. Speaking to people in Brazil, Chile, Tunisia, Egypt, Bahrain, Yemen, Turkey, Hong Kong, and South Korea, he wanted to find out why, in so many places where people had taken to the streets in support of more freedom, or a fairer distribution of wealth, the ultimate outcome had been forms of authoritarian and right-wing reaction. Was there anything, he asked former activists, youвҖҷd say to a young person fighting for change in the world right now?

DonвҖҷt give up, was one frequent piece of advice. But so was something equally important: think about what comes next. Again and again, the former activists told Bevins they had been naive in thinking that just because they were in the right, things would turn out for the best. Mahmoud Salem, an Egyptian blogger who took part in the 2011 Tahrir Square protests that brought down the regime of Hosni Mubarak, recalled the final battle scene from вҖҳThe Lord of the Rings,вҖҷ when the monstrous villain Sauron is defeated once and for all. Why, Salem now wonders, did they think the Arab Spring would be like the movies? Why didnвҖҷt they have a plan for what came next? As two Brazilians who took part in the mass protests in their country in 2013 told Bevins, вҖңThere is no such thing as a political vacuum.вҖқ Either you fill the gap, or someone else will come along and do it for you. (In Brazil, as we now know, that someone was Jair Bolsonaro.) In April 2026, Hungarians rightly celebrated the downfall of Viktor OrbГЎn, EuropeвҖҷs longest-serving far-right populist. But for Hungary, the task of repairing the countryвҖҷs damaged institutions вҖ“ not to mention dealing with the economic discontent that helped do for OrbГЎn вҖ“ has only just begun.

Yet defeat isnвҖҷt the end, either. In 2025, people who had campaigned to kick Trump out of power in 2020 had to pick themselves up and start again. Although Trump began his second term in far more menacing form, the opposition is finding ways to turn this against him. The anti-ICE protests in Minnesota at the turn of the year, for instance, were partly the result of years of patient organizing. Sarah Jaffe, a journalist and author who covers workersвҖҷ rights, points out that these efforts came on the back of existing campaigns for causes like better working conditions at the local Amazon distribution center, tenantsвҖҷ rights, and free school meals for undocumented migrants. вҖңThese organizations are used to working together and responding quickly to crisis; they have built bonds of trust,вҖқ Jaffe writes, вҖңand they have learned to align their goals without needing perfect agreement.вҖқ

This sort of campaigning isnвҖҷt just a necessary defense against far-right intimidation. Ultimately, itвҖҷs the only way we will reach better politics. If mainstream politicians вҖ“ who are only as good as we make them вҖ“ are not talking about the things that would really make our communities stronger and more secure, then we need to take the lead.

If you want to speak out, to oppose the far rightвҖҷs racism or the excuses people make for it, do it. Your voice is legitimate.

For all the essentialist talk from right-wing nationalists about culture and identity, one thing they understand very well is that nations are made, not born. ThatвҖҷs why, in the UK, where I live, far-right activists spent last summer attaching national flags to lampposts around the UK and encouraging other people to do the same, not as a neutral expression of pride, but as a deliberate attempt to tie peopleвҖҷs sense of home to their false claim that the UK is under invasion. That the country is, in the words of Jim Ratcliffe вҖ“ BritainвҖҷs richest man, who moved to Monaco in 2020, reportedly to save billions in taxes вҖ“ being вҖңcolonized by migrants.вҖқ

Many of us do not agree. Take the example of Hannah White, a co-founder of the Sound Lounge, a popular local music venue in Sutton, south London. In the summer of 2025, she asked a group of men wearing masks not to attach a Union Jack to the lamp post outside the venue. They threatened her. WhiteвҖҷs response was to invite customers to bring in other flags of countries and causes that meant something to them. She even exchanged messages online with a less hostile local supporter of the flag-raising. вҖңI donвҖҷt want to be angry against people who are feeling like they want to celebrate their flag,вҖқ White later said. вҖңItвҖҷs just, how do we celebrate everyone?вҖқ

A few days after White was threatened, the venueвҖҷs windows were smashed with a hammer, in the dead of night. She kept going. Hundreds of people contributed to a crowdfunding campaign to repair the damage. Challenging the far rightвҖҷs narrative вҖ“ and refusing to back down, without bitterness or hate вҖ“ was an incredibly brave thing to do. But really, do we have any other choice?

This is an edited extract from Daniel TrillingвҖҷs вҖҳIf We Tolerate This: How the British Establishment Made the Far Right Respectable,вҖҷ out now in the UK by Picador, an imprint of Pan Macmillan. Copyright 2026.

Daniel Trilling is a journalist and author based in London, UK. He writes about nationalism, migration, and human rights for publications including the London Review of Books, The Guardian, and the New York Times. His work has been shortlisted for the Orwell Prize, the Political Book Awards, and the Bread and Roses Award for Radical Publishing.
